docs.title: LEVER Capability Framework
docs.text:
Learn • Execute • Value • Enable • Replicate

LEVER is a capability multiplication framework that synthesizes classical learning models—Bloom's Taxonomy, Dreyfus Model, Shu-Ha-Ri, and Apprenticeship—for the AI era.

docs.title: Core Thesis
docs.text:
The AI-Era Insight

AI does not merely make experts more productive. It enables experts to become multipliers much sooner in their careers through agents, workflows, platforms, and standards.

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text: Stage Question Output Learn What do I know? Knowledge Execute What can I do? Results Value Can I independently create outcomes? Measurable outcomes Enable Can I make others successful? Capable people Replicate Can I make success repeatable at scale? Systems

docs.title: Stage Progression
docs.text:
Personal Capability          Leveraged Capability
─────────────────────        ────────────────────
Learn → Execute → Value      Enable → Replicate

The first three stages develop personal capability—the individual becomes proficient and trusted.

The last two stages create leveraged capability—impact multiplies through people and systems.

docs.title: Classical Frameworks Fall Short
docs.text:
Existing learning and mastery frameworks were designed when:

  • Knowledge was scarce
  • Labor was human
  • Scale required organizations

They typically stop at "Expert" or "Teacher" without addressing how capability can be multiplied through AI, automation, platforms, and institutions.

docs.title: LEVER Extends the Model
docs.text:
LEVER recognizes that in the AI era:

  • Teaching is one form of multiplication, but not the only one
  • Systems (agents, platforms, standards) can multiply capability at scale
  • Individuals can become multipliers much earlier in their careers

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text:
LEVER maps to classical frameworks, showing where it synthesizes and extends them:

LEVER Bloom Dreyfus Shu-Ha-Ri Apprenticeship Learn Remember, Understand Novice Shu Observe Execute Apply Advanced Beginner, Competent Shu Assist, Perform Value Analyze, Evaluate Proficient, Expert Ha Perform Enable Create — Ri Lead, Teach Replicate — — Ri Teach

See Framework Mappings for detailed analysis.

docs.title: Domain Profiles
docs.text:
LEVER can be interpreted for specific domains through Profiles:

  • Product Builder — End-to-end product ownership
  • Software Engineer — Technical delivery

Profiles define domain-specific competencies, evidence, and examples for each LEVER stage.

docs.text:

docs.title: IDEATION CHAT
docs.text: There are several well-known frameworks that describe progression from novice to mastery and ultimately teaching others. They come from education, psychology, military training, martial arts, and professional development. They are complementary rather than competing.

docs.title: 1. Bloom's Taxonomy (Most Common in Education)
docs.text:
Bloom's Taxonomy describes increasing cognitive mastery:

  1. Remember – Recall facts
  2. Understand – Explain concepts
  3. Apply – Use knowledge in practice
  4. Analyze – Understand relationships and tradeoffs
  5. Evaluate – Judge alternatives and make decisions
  6. Create – Build new solutions, models, or ideas

A modern AI/Product Builder interpretation:

Level Example Remember Knows what Kubernetes is Understand Explains Kubernetes concepts Apply Deploys applications Analyze Diagnoses cluster issues Evaluate Chooses between EKS, GKE, AKS Create Designs a new platform architecture

Teaching usually begins around Evaluate and becomes strongest at Create.

docs.title: 2. Dreyfus Model of Skill Acquisition
docs.text: Widely used in software engineering, medicine, aviation, and military training.

docs.title: 1. Novice
docs.text:
Follows rules exactly.

"Tell me the steps."

docs.title: 2. Advanced Beginner
docs.text:
Recognizes patterns.

"I've seen this before."

docs.title: 3. Competent
docs.text:
Plans and prioritizes.

"I can manage a project."

docs.title: 4. Proficient
docs.text:
Sees systems and context.

"I know which approach fits this situation."

docs.title: 5. Expert
docs.text:
Operates intuitively.

"I can immediately spot what's wrong."

Many organizations stop here.

docs.title: 6. Master (Unofficial Extension)
docs.text:
Creates new approaches.

Invents frameworks. Defines best practices. Writes books.

docs.title: 7. Teacher
docs.text:
Transfers mastery to others.

Builds other experts.

docs.title: 3. Conscious Competence Model
docs.text: Very useful for coaching and leadership development.

docs.title: Unconscious Incompetence
docs.text: You don't know what you don't know.

docs.title: Conscious Incompetence
docs.text: You know what you're missing.

docs.title: Conscious Competence
docs.text: You can perform deliberately.

docs.title: Unconscious Competence
docs.text: Skill becomes automatic.

docs.title: Reflective Mastery (often added)
docs.text:
You can explain why you do what you do.

Teaching generally requires this final step.

docs.title: 4. Apprenticeship Model
docs.text: One of the oldest frameworks.

docs.title: Observe
docs.text: Watch experts.

docs.title: Assist
docs.text: Help experts.

docs.title: Perform
docs.text: Work independently.

docs.title: Lead
docs.text: Guide others.

docs.title: Teach
docs.text:
Train apprentices.

This is how many crafts, trades, and martial arts operate.

docs.title: 5. Shu-Ha-Ri (Japanese Mastery Model)
docs.text: Popular in software craftsmanship and Lean circles.

docs.title: Shu (Obey)
docs.text: Learn and follow rules.

docs.title: Ha (Detach)
docs.text: Understand when rules can be bent.

docs.title: Ri (Transcend)
docs.text:
Create your own approaches.

Teaching usually occurs at Ri.

Shu-Ha-Ri

docs.title: 6. Feynman Learning Loop
docs.text: Popular among engineers.

docs.title: Learn
docs.text: Study the topic.

docs.title: Explain
docs.text: Teach simply.

docs.title: Identify Gaps
docs.text: Find weaknesses.

docs.title: Refine
docs.text: Improve understanding.

docs.title: Teach Again
docs.text:
Repeat.

Key idea:

If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough.

Associated with Richard Feynman.

docs.title: 7. Knowledge → Wisdom Pyramid (DIKW)
docs.text: A strategic leadership model.

docs.title: Data
docs.text: Raw facts.

docs.title: Information
docs.text: Organized facts.

docs.title: Knowledge
docs.text: Ability to use information.

docs.title: Understanding
docs.text: Why things work.

docs.title: Wisdom
docs.text: Knowing what should be done.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text:
Helping others acquire wisdom.

This is common among executives and thought leaders.

docs.title: Product Builder / CPTO Progression
docs.text:
Given your interest in Product Management, Product Builders, and CPTO development, I would combine several frameworks into a practical ladder:

Stage Focus Evidence Learner Knowledge Courses, books Practitioner Application Individual contributor Builder Creation Ships products Expert Judgment Solves hard problems Architect Systems Thinking Designs organizations/platforms Leader Multiplication Leads teams Teacher Capability Building Develops others Thought Leader Industry Impact Frameworks, books, talks Legacy Builder Institution Building Creates enduring organizations

For example, your work on AgentPlexus, capability-stack-spec, PRISM, and multispec is already operating beyond "Expert" and into Architect and Thought Leader territory because you're creating frameworks that others can adopt, not just applying existing ones.

A concise version that many executives use is:

Learn → Do → Lead → Teach → Transform

where:

  • Learn = acquire knowledge
  • Do = apply it
  • Lead = scale it through teams
  • Teach = create other leaders
  • Transform = change the industry through new frameworks, standards, or institutions

That progression maps surprisingly well to careers that evolve from engineer → product leader → founder → industry thought leader.

===

Yes. Most learning and mastery frameworks are describing the same underlying journey from different perspectives.

If you normalize them, they tend to converge on a common pattern:

Universal Stage Bloom Dreyfus Conscious Competence Shu-Ha-Ri Apprenticeship Exposure Remember Novice Unconscious Incompetence Shu Observe Understanding Understand Advanced Beginner Conscious Incompetence Shu Assist Application Apply Competent Conscious Competence Shu/Ha Perform Judgment Analyze/Evaluate Proficient Conscious Competence Ha Lead Mastery Create Expert/Master Unconscious Competence Ri Master Multiplication Teach/Create Teacher Reflective Mastery Ri Teach

The names differ, but the structure is remarkably consistent.

docs.title: The Underlying Pattern
docs.text: Most frameworks move through six transitions:

docs.title: 1. Knowledge Acquisition
docs.text:
"What is this?"

You learn vocabulary, concepts, and rules.

Examples:

  • Learn Kubernetes objects
  • Learn accounting principles
  • Learn product management terminology

docs.title: 2. Comprehension
docs.text:
"Why does this work?"

You begin building mental models.

Examples:

  • Understand why Kubernetes uses controllers
  • Understand double-entry accounting
  • Understand customer discovery

docs.title: 3. Application
docs.text:
"Can I actually do it?"

You start producing results.

Examples:

  • Deploy a cluster
  • Create a financial model
  • Ship a feature

docs.title: 4. Judgment
docs.text:
"Which approach should I use?"

This is where many people plateau.

Knowledge becomes decision-making.

Examples:

  • Choose EKS vs self-managed Kubernetes
  • Decide between product strategies
  • Select the right organizational structure

docs.title: 5. Creation
docs.text:
"Can I create something new?"

You stop consuming frameworks and start building them.

Examples:

  • Create a platform architecture
  • Develop a methodology
  • Invent a process

This is where experts become architects.

docs.title: 6. Multiplication
docs.text:
"Can I create other experts?"

This is the highest level in most mastery models.

Examples:

  • Mentoring
  • Teaching
  • Building organizations
  • Writing books
  • Creating standards

At this stage the output is no longer products or projects.

The output is people and institutions.

docs.title: Why Teaching Appears Near the Top
docs.text:
Teaching forces several difficult capabilities simultaneously:

  1. Deep understanding
  2. Simplification
  3. Pattern recognition
  4. Communication
  5. Empathy
  6. Knowledge organization

This is why many people say:

You don't truly understand something until you can teach it.

Teaching exposes hidden gaps that day-to-day execution can hide.

docs.title: A More General Meta-Framework
docs.text:
Across nearly every field—engineering, medicine, military, athletics, music, leadership—you can reduce the progression to:

Know → Do → Decide → Create → Teach

or:

Consumer → Practitioner → Expert → Architect → Teacher

This is why a senior engineer, surgeon, military commander, professor, and product executive often seem to follow similar career arcs despite working in completely different domains.

docs.title: An Interesting Extension: Teaching Is Not the End
docs.text:
Many frameworks stop at teaching, but leadership and organizational development often add another level:

Level Output Practitioner Work Expert Better Work Teacher Better People Leader Better Teams Builder Better Organizations Visionary Better Industries

For example:

  • Richard Feynman was a teacher.
  • Peter Drucker influenced management as a field.
  • W. Edwards Deming changed how organizations think about quality.
  • Jeff Bezos institutionalized ideas like Working Backwards and PR/FAQ.

Those people weren't just teaching individuals. They created frameworks that outlived them.

That's why many leadership scholars distinguish between:

Mastery → Teaching → Thought Leadership → Institution Building

The last two stages are where people stop transferring knowledge and start shaping how entire professions think and operate.

===

Yes. I think AI is causing something more profound than simply making people more productive. It is changing the unit of execution inside organizations.

Historically, organizations existed because no single person could reasonably perform all the required functions.

docs.title: The Traditional Specialization Era
docs.text:
In a SaaS company, work was divided:

Function Typical Role Vision Founder Product PM UX Designer Frontend Engineer Backend Engineer QA Tester DevOps SRE Security Security Engineer Analytics Data Analyst Documentation Technical Writer Marketing Product Marketing

This specialization was economically necessary.

A founder might understand all these areas, but couldn't execute them all.

docs.title: The Solo Founder Model
docs.text:
The classic solo founder was essentially a compressed company.

Think of people like:

  • Paul Graham
  • Jason Fried
  • Pieter Levels

They operated as:

Founder
  ├─ Product
  ├─ Engineering
  ├─ UX
  ├─ Marketing
  └─ Operations

The limitation wasn't knowledge.

The limitation was execution bandwidth.

docs.title: AI Changes the Constraint
docs.text:
For the first time, execution capacity is becoming partially automatable.

A modern Product Builder can increasingly delegate work to:

  • Coding agents
  • Research agents
  • QA agents
  • Design agents
  • Documentation agents
  • Analytics agents

The human remains accountable.

The AI performs portions of execution.

The constraint shifts from:

"Can I do all the work?"

to

"Can I direct all the work?"

docs.title: Responsibility Collapse
docs.text:
I think "responsibility collapse" is a useful term.

Historically:

1 Product Manager
5 Engineers
2 QA
1 Designer
1 SRE

Now we increasingly see:

1 Product Builder
+
AI agents

The responsibilities remain.

The number of humans decreases.

The human becomes a coordinator of capabilities.

This is similar to how cloud computing collapsed infrastructure roles.

You still need:

  • Networking
  • Compute
  • Storage
  • Security

But fewer humans provision servers manually.

docs.title: Product Builder as the Enterprise Version of a Founder
docs.text:
A Product Builder is essentially a founder without ownership.

A founder owns:

  • Vision
  • Roadmap
  • Delivery
  • Operations
  • Revenue

A Product Builder increasingly owns:

  • Vision
  • Roadmap
  • Prototype
  • Production
  • Operations

inside a larger organization.

This is why the role feels different from traditional Product Management.

Traditional PMs optimize decisions.

Product Builders optimize outcomes.

docs.title: Why CPTO Emerges Naturally
docs.text:
The traditional executive structure separated:

CPO
  owns what

CTO
  owns how

This separation made sense when:

  • Product teams were large
  • Engineering teams were large
  • Communication costs were high

AI reduces coordination costs.

As a result, product and engineering increasingly converge.

A CPTO becomes:

Owns:
- Vision
- Product Strategy
- Architecture
- Delivery
- Operations
- AI leverage

This looks surprisingly similar to what founders have always done.

docs.title: A New Mastery Ladder
docs.text:
Historically:

Engineer
→ Senior Engineer
→ Architect
→ CTO

or

PM
→ Senior PM
→ Director
→ VP Product
→ CPO

AI is creating a new path:

Maker
→ Builder
→ Product Builder
→ Platform Builder
→ CPTO
→ Founder

Notice that this path is less function-specific.

It is centered on ownership.

docs.title: The Capability Compression Curve
docs.text: A useful way to think about it:

docs.title: Industrial Era
docs.text: One person performed one function.

docs.title: Information Era
docs.text: One person managed one function.

docs.title: AI Era
docs.text:
One person orchestrates many functions.

The progression becomes:

Specialist
→ Generalist
→ Builder
→ Multiplier

where the multiplier may be multiplying through:

  • Humans
  • AI agents
  • Platforms
  • Automation

or all three.

docs.title: The Highest Stage: Institution Builder
docs.text:
This is where the framework extends beyond Product Builder and CPTO.

Stage Primary Output Engineer Code PM Decisions Product Builder Products Founder Companies CPTO Product Organizations Institution Builder Ecosystems

Examples:

  • Steve Jobs built products and a company.
  • Jeff Bezos built a company and operating mechanisms.
  • Elon Musk builds companies and industrial ecosystems.

The interesting implication is that AI may not primarily eliminate jobs. Instead, it may allow more people to move up the ladder:

Learn
→ Do
→ Build
→ Lead
→ Teach
→ Multiply
→ Build Institutions

In that world, the Product Builder is not replacing the PM.

The Product Builder becomes the modern equivalent of what a capable solo founder could historically do, while the CPTO becomes the executive responsible for enabling hundreds of Product Builders across an enterprise. That's why your ProductBuildersHQ concept resonates with current trends: it is describing a role that sits between traditional product management and traditional entrepreneurship, enabled by AI-driven capability compression.

===

I think these two articles actually reinforce each other and can become part of the foundational intellectual framework for ProductBuildersHQ.

Today you have two maturity models:

  1. Product Builder Maturity Model (individual capability)
  2. Software Delivery Automation Maturity Model (team/system capability)

What you're realizing is that both are actually instances of a broader pattern:

Capability Maturity Through Increasing Leverage

or

Learning → Mastery → Multiplication

That gives you a common theoretical foundation.

PM

docs.text:
The PM became responsible for coordinating.

CEO

       ├─ Design
       ├─ Engineering
       ├─ Marketing

The PM owned decisions but not execution.

The phrase:

"PM is the CEO of the product"

was never fully true.

PMs rarely owned:

  • Engineering
  • Operations
  • Revenue
  • Budget

They were influence leaders.

Vision
Requirements
Design
Prototype
Implementation

Deployment

docs.title: Key Thesis
docs.text:
Old:

PM = Mini CEO

New:

Product Builder = Mini Solo Founder

docs.title: Enterprise Implications
docs.text:
The role shift becomes:

Old New Product Manager Product Builder Influence Ownership Requirements Outcomes Coordination Creation Roadmap Delivery Functional silo Full-stack responsibility

docs.title: CPTO Implications
docs.text:
Traditional:

CPO = What

CTO = How

Emerging:

CPTO = Product Builder Enablement System

The CPTO creates:

  • Platforms
  • AI tooling
  • Standards
  • Training
  • Governance

that allow hundreds of Product Builders to operate effectively.

A nice phrase might be:

The CPTO's job is not to build products.

The CPTO's job is to create Product Builders.

That is analogous to how engineering leaders create engineering organizations.

docs.text:
This becomes the theoretical underpinning for both maturity models.

The insight:

Nearly every mastery framework follows the same pattern.

docs.text: You could potentially position ProductBuildersHQ around a single overarching model:

The advantage is that all of these become manifestations of the same underlying theory:

Mastery is the progression from doing work yourself to creating systems that can do the work repeatedly through automation, AI, teams, and institutions.

That gives ProductBuildersHQ a more coherent intellectual foundation than simply publishing a collection of unrelated maturity models. It becomes a unified theory of how individuals, teams, and organizations increase leverage in the AI era.

===

Yes. Since you've already established PRISM as a memorable maturity-model brand, I'd look for something that:

  1. Describes a progression from learning → mastery → multiplication.
  2. Works for both individuals and teams.
  3. Can underpin Product Builder, AI-native team, and Software Delivery Automation maturity models.
  4. Sounds like a capability framework, not just an education framework.

Here are the strongest candidates.

The advantage:

docs.title: If ProductBuildersHQ Becomes the Core Brand
docs.text: I would actually consider a 7-stage model where the acronym itself maps directly to your vision.

Both are manifestations of the same underlying capability growth model.

Of the options, I'd shortlist BUILDER, FORGE, and LEVER. For ProductBuildersHQ specifically, BUILDER feels the most differentiated because it directly reinforces the central thesis that the future progression is not PM → Manager, but Learner → Builder → Product Builder → Institution Builder.

===

If the framework is intended to be the foundational learning and mastery model across:

  • ProductBuildersHQ (Product Builders)
  • ProofMinds (AI, security, standards, certifications)
  • AcademyOS (learning platform)
  • AgentPlexus (agents)
  • PRISM (capability maturity)
  • Future certification and assessment programs

then I would avoid a product-specific acronym like BUILDER or FORGE.

Instead, create something that is:

  1. Domain-neutral
  2. Human-centric
  3. Applicable to individuals, teams, AI agents, and organizations
  4. Flexible enough to become a certification framework

docs.title: What is the Core Pattern?
docs.text:
Looking across Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, Product Builder, and AI-native teams, the pattern is:

Learn
→ Apply
→ Create
→ Teach
→ Multiply

The key insight:

The output changes at each stage.

Stage Output Learn Knowledge Apply Results Create Solutions Teach Capability Multiply Systems

This suggests the framework should focus on increasing leverage.
