docs.title: ELEVATE
docs.text:
Explore Learn Execute Validate Advise Transform Expand

Progression:

Explorer
→ Learner
→ Practitioner
→ Expert
→ Mentor
→ Leader
→ Institution Builder

Very broad applicability.

docs.title: Candidate 4: PRISM Companion Model
docs.text:
Since PRISM already exists, consider a companion framework.

PRISM answers:

How mature is a capability?

New framework answers:

How mature is a learner?

Example:

docs.title: APEX
docs.text:
Awareness Practice Expertise X-Multiplier

Expanded:

Stage Description Awareness Learn Practice Apply Expertise Create Multiplier Teach and Scale

Simple and memorable.

docs.title: Candidate 5: Catalyst Framework
docs.text:
This is the one I would seriously consider.

Why?

The common thread across ProductBuildersHQ, AcademyOS, and ProofMinds is not learning.

It's transformation.

You are trying to help people become something more capable.

docs.title: CATALYST
docs.text:
Curiosity Acquisition Training Application Leadership Yield Scaling Transformation

Progression:

Level Output Curiosity Interest Acquisition Knowledge Training Skill Application Results Leadership Team impact Yield Repeatable outcomes Scaling Systems Transformation Institutional impact

This works for:

  • Individual learners
  • Product Builders
  • AI-native teams
  • Organizations
  • Certifications
  • Capability maturity

docs.title: A More Fundamental Insight
docs.text: You may actually want two orthogonal frameworks:

docs.title: 1. Capability Maturity (PRISM-style)
docs.text:
Measures:

How advanced is the capability?

Examples:

  • Product Building
  • Software Delivery
  • Security
  • AI Adoption

docs.title: 2. Mastery Progression (new framework)
docs.text:
Measures:

How advanced is the learner?

Examples:

  • Student
  • Product Builder
  • Engineer
  • Team
  • Organization

Think:

PRISM = Capability maturity

ASCEND (or CATALYST) = Learner maturity

One measures the thing.

The other measures the people building the thing.

For your ecosystem, ASCEND is probably the cleanest and most reusable name because it is domain-neutral, positive, certification-friendly, and naturally conveys progression from learner to multiplier without tying the framework to any specific profession or technology.

===

I would be cautious about creating an entirely new learning framework unless you are genuinely contributing a new insight.

One of the reasons SOLID, SMART, RACI, DACI, OKR, V2MOM, and PRISM work is that they aren't just acronyms—they encode a useful mental model that is either:

  • Easier to remember than the underlying theory, or
  • A genuine synthesis of multiple existing ideas.

For ProductBuildersHQ, ProofMinds, and AcademyOS, I think the opportunity is not to replace Bloom, Dreyfus, or Shu-Ha-Ri.

The opportunity is to unify them.

docs.title: What Existing Frameworks Already Do Well
Bloom
docs.text:
Answers:

What level of cognitive mastery have I achieved?

Remember
Understand
Apply
Analyze
Evaluate
Create

Excellent for curriculum design.

docs.title: Dreyfus
docs.text:
Answers:

How independently can I perform?

Novice
Advanced Beginner
Competent
Proficient
Expert

Excellent for career ladders.

docs.title: Shu-Ha-Ri
docs.text:
Answers:

How dependent am I on rules?

Shu
Ha
Ri

Excellent for craftsmanship.

docs.title: Apprenticeship
docs.text:
Answers:

How much responsibility can I own?

Observe
Assist
Perform
Lead
Teach

Excellent for professional growth.

docs.title: What They All Miss
docs.text:
None of them were designed for:

  • AI-assisted builders
  • Product Builders
  • Solo founders
  • AI-native teams
  • Institution building
  • Capability multiplication

Most stop at:

Expert

or

Teacher

The modern AI era introduces a new stage:

Multiplier

because one person can increasingly direct:

  • AI agents
  • Automations
  • Teams
  • Platforms

This is arguably the new insight.

docs.title: A Synthesis Framework
docs.text:
Rather than replacing Bloom/Dreyfus/Shu-Ha-Ri, consider creating a synthesis model.

For example:

docs.title: SCALE
docs.text:
Study Competence Autonomy Leadership Expansion

SCALE Bloom Dreyfus Apprenticeship Study Remember, Understand Novice Observe Competence Apply Competent Assist Autonomy Analyze, Evaluate Proficient Perform Leadership Create Expert Lead Expansion Beyond Expert Master/Teacher Teach

The value isn't the acronym.

The value is the mapping.

docs.title: Another Option: BUILD
docs.text: Since ProductBuildersHQ is a major initiative.

docs.title: BUILD
docs.text:
Begin Understand Implement Lead Duplicate

Where:

Begin
  ↓
Understand
  ↓
Implement
  ↓
Lead
  ↓
Duplicate

The last step is important.

Historically:

Learn
→ Do
→ Teach

AI era:

Learn
→ Do
→ Lead
→ Multiply

The word "Duplicate" is really:

Create additional capability through people, AI, automation, or institutions.

docs.title: What I Would Do
docs.text:
For ProductBuildersHQ and AcademyOS, I would not position the framework as:

"A replacement for Bloom or Dreyfus."

Instead:

"A modern synthesis of Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, and AI-native work."

Something like:

docs.title: SCALE Framework
docs.text:
S - Study
C - Competence
A - Autonomy
L - Leadership
E - Expansion

And explicitly show mappings.

SCALE Bloom Dreyfus Shu-Ha-Ri Study Remember, Understand Novice Shu Competence Apply Competent Shu Autonomy Analyze, Evaluate Proficient Ha Leadership Create Expert Ri Expansion Teach, Multiply Master Ri

This gives you three advantages:

  1. Immediate credibility (built on established theory).
  2. Memorable acronym (like SOLID).
  3. Modern extension (adds AI-era multiplication and institution building).

Of the names we've discussed, SCALE is probably the strongest because it works equally well for:

  • Individual learners
  • Product Builders
  • AI-native teams
  • Organizations
  • Capability maturity models
  • AcademyOS certifications

And its core message is exactly what your recent work has been about:

The goal of mastery is not simply expertise. The goal is increasing leverage and scale.

===

Yes—but only if you're solving a problem the existing frameworks do not solve.

That's the test I would use.

If your framework is merely:

Bloom + Dreyfus + Shu-Ha-Ri
with a new acronym

then practitioners will rightly ask:

Why not just use Bloom or Dreyfus?

The answer has to be stronger than "because acronyms are memorable."

docs.title: The Real Question
docs.text:
The question isn't:

Should we create a new learning framework?

The question is:

Has the AI era introduced a new stage of mastery that existing frameworks don't adequately describe?

I think the answer is yes.

docs.title: Where Existing Frameworks Came From
docs.text:
Notice when these frameworks were created:

Framework Era Apprenticeship Pre-industrial Bloom 1950s Dreyfus 1980s Shu-Ha-Ri Pre-modern martial arts

They all assume something important:

Humans are the primary unit of execution.

The journey is:

Learn
→ Practice
→ Master
→ Teach

That was sufficient when:

  • Knowledge was scarce
  • Labor was human
  • Scale required organizations

docs.title: What Changed?
docs.text: AI changes the relationship between:

docs.title: Knowledge
docs.text: and

docs.title: Execution
docs.text:
Historically:

Knowledge
→ Human effort
→ Results

Today:

Knowledge
→ AI orchestration
→ Results

This creates a new capability.

Not teaching.

Not mastery.

docs.title: Multiplication.
docs.text:
One person can now direct:

  • AI agents
  • Automated workflows
  • Digital workers
  • Large codebases
  • Small teams

at a scale previously requiring many people.

docs.title: The Missing Stage
docs.text:
Most frameworks stop here:

Novice
→ Competent
→ Expert
→ Teacher

The AI age introduces:

Novice
→ Competent
→ Expert
→ Teacher
→ Multiplier

where the output is no longer knowledge transfer.

The output becomes capability creation.

docs.title: Product Builder Example
docs.text:
Traditional PM:

Learn PM
→ Execute PM
→ Become Senior PM
→ Mentor PMs

AI-age Product Builder:

Learn
→ Build
→ Ship
→ Lead
→ Orchestrate Humans + AI
→ Create Builders

The latter is not fully represented by Bloom or Dreyfus.

docs.title: Software Delivery Example
docs.text:
Traditional:

Developer
→ Senior
→ Architect
→ Manager
→ Director

AI-native:

Developer
→ Builder
→ Product Builder
→ AI Team Lead
→ Capability Multiplier

Again, there is a shift from:

docs.title: Doing
docs.text: to

docs.title: What I Would Recommend
docs.text:
Don't present your framework as a replacement.

Present it as an extension.

For example:

docs.title: Existing Frameworks
docs.text:
• Bloom explains cognitive mastery.
  • Dreyfus explains skill acquisition.
  • Shu-Ha-Ri explains independence from rules.
  • Apprenticeship explains responsibility growth.

docs.title: New Framework
docs.text:
Explains:

Capability multiplication in the AI era.

That is genuinely different.

docs.title: The Strongest Positioning
docs.text: Imagine ProductBuildersHQ publishing:

docs.title: AI-Age Learning Model
docs.text:
Learn
→ Apply
→ Create
→ Lead
→ Teach
→ Multiply

Then explicitly mapping:

AI-Age Model Bloom Dreyfus Shu-Ha-Ri Learn Remember/Understand Novice Shu Apply Apply Competent Shu Create Analyze/Create Proficient Ha Lead Evaluate/Create Expert Ri Teach Beyond Bloom Teacher Ri Multiply Not covered Not covered Not covered

That last row is where your contribution lives.

docs.title: Why This Fits ProductBuildersHQ
docs.text:
Your recent work consistently revolves around the same idea:

  • Product Builder vs PM
  • Solo Founder in the Enterprise
  • AI-native teams
  • AWS AI DLC mob programming
  • Agent orchestration
  • CPTO evolution
  • Responsibility collapse
  • Capability ladders

These are all manifestations of one phenomenon:

AI enables capability multiplication.

That's not what Bloom was trying to explain.

That's not what Dreyfus was trying to explain.

So I would not create a framework because you want an acronym.

I would create one if you believe:

The AI era requires extending classical mastery models with one or more new stages centered on leverage, multiplication, and institution building.

If that's the thesis, then the framework has a reason to exist, and the acronym becomes a packaging mechanism for a genuinely new idea rather than a rebranding exercise.

===

Actually, several existing frameworks do include mentoring, teaching, or mastery beyond execution, but they usually treat it differently than what we're discussing.

The nuance is important.

docs.title: Apprenticeship Absolutely Includes Teaching
docs.text:
The classic apprenticeship model is often:

Apprentice
→ Journeyman
→ Master

The Master is expected to:

  • Teach apprentices
  • Preserve the craft
  • Certify competence
  • Advance the profession

In some ways, apprenticeship gets closest to your Product Builder thinking.

The limitation is that it was designed around human-to-human skill transfer.

docs.title: Dreyfus Implicitly Includes Mentoring
docs.text:
The original Dreyfus model stops at:

Novice
→ Advanced Beginner
→ Competent
→ Proficient
→ Expert

It does not formally include Teacher or Master.

However, many organizations extend it:

Expert
→ Master
→ Teacher

This is common in:

  • Medicine
  • Engineering
  • Military
  • Consulting

The "Master" level is usually unofficial.

docs.title: Bloom Doesn't Really Focus on Teaching
docs.text:
Bloom is different.

Bloom is about:

Remember
Understand
Apply
Analyze
Evaluate
Create

Teaching is not a stage.

Teaching may happen at any stage.

Bloom focuses on cognitive complexity, not professional maturity.

This is one reason Bloom isn't sufficient as a career ladder.

docs.title: Shu-Ha-Ri Includes Teaching Indirectly
docs.text:
At Ri:

Shu
→ Ha
→ Ri

the practitioner transcends the system.

Traditionally, many Ri-level practitioners become teachers.

But teaching isn't explicitly represented.

Ri is more:

Create your own style.

than

Develop other practitioners.

docs.title: What Existing Frameworks Usually Stop At
docs.text: Most frameworks culminate in one of three endpoints:

docs.title: Endpoint 1: Expertise
docs.text:
Dreyfus

Expert

docs.title: Endpoint 2: Creation
docs.text:
Bloom

Create

docs.title: Endpoint 3: Master
docs.text:
Apprenticeship

Master

None of them were primarily designed to model:

Expert
→ Teacher
→ Leader
→ Institution Builder

which is often what happens in real careers.

docs.title: Leadership Frameworks Go Further
docs.text:
If you look outside learning theory and into leadership theory, you start seeing the stages you're talking about.

For example:

docs.title: John Maxwell
docs.text:
Leadership progression:

Position
→ Permission
→ Production
→ People Development
→ Pinnacle

Notice:

People Development means creating leaders.

docs.title: Leadership Pipeline
docs.text:
Leadership moves from:

Individual Contributor
→ Manager
→ Manager of Managers
→ Functional Leader
→ Enterprise Leader

Again, the output changes from work to people.

docs.title: The Real Gap May Not Be Teaching
docs.text:
This is where I would refine the earlier argument.

The gap is probably not:

Existing Frameworks
❌ Don't include teaching

because some clearly do.

The gap is:

Existing Frameworks
❌ Don't clearly model capability multiplication

Teaching is one form of multiplication.

But not the only one.

docs.title: AI Changes the Meaning of Multiplication
docs.text:
Historically:

Learn
→ Do
→ Teach

because the only way to scale capability was through people.

Today:

Learn
→ Do
→ Teach
→ Multiply

where multiplication may occur through:

  • Mentoring people
  • Leading teams
  • Creating processes
  • Building platforms
  • Building standards
  • Creating AI agents
  • Creating automation

This is fundamentally different.

docs.title: Product Builder Example
docs.text:
Consider a Product Builder who creates:

  • a PRD template,
  • an AI workflow,
  • a code generator,
  • an evaluation rubric.

That person may have multiplied capability across 500 engineers.

They may never directly mentor anyone.

Traditional frameworks would struggle to classify that impact.

docs.title: A Better Framing
docs.text:
Rather than saying:

Existing frameworks stop at teaching.

I would say:

Existing frameworks were created when teaching was the primary mechanism for scaling capability.

In the AI era, capability can also be scaled through automation, agents, platforms, standards, and institutions.

That's a stronger and more defensible position.

So if ProductBuildersHQ creates a framework, the novel contribution is probably not:

Expert → Teacher

because apprenticeship traditions have recognized that for centuries.

The novel contribution is:

Expert
→ Teacher
→ Multiplier
→ Institution Builder

where "multiplier" includes humans, AI, automation, processes, platforms, and organizations—not just direct mentoring. That aligns much more closely with your Product Builder, CPTO, Solo Founder, AgentPlexus, and Software Delivery Automation thinking.

===

Yes, but I would define Multiplier more broadly.

An AI tool builder or agent orchestrator is one type of multiplier, not the entirety of the concept.

Think of it as:

Expert = Does the work

Teacher = Enables another person to do the work

Multiplier = Creates a system that enables many people and/or AI to do the work

docs.title: Historical Examples of Multipliers
docs.text: Before AI existed, multipliers already existed.

docs.title: Teacher
docs.text:
A teacher might train:

1 → 20 people

docs.title: Author
docs.text:
A book might reach:

1 → 10,000 people

docs.title: Framework Creator
docs.text: Someone like W. Edwards Deming created quality frameworks that influenced millions of workers.

docs.title: Platform Builder
docs.text:
Someone at Amazon Web Services creates a service that enables thousands of teams.

These are all multipliers.

docs.title: AI Creates New Forms of Multiplication
docs.text: Now the multiplier can create:

docs.title: AI Tool
docs.text:
Person
  ↓
Tool
  ↓
100 users

Example:

  • Internal code generator
  • Security review tool
  • PRD generator

docs.title: AI Agent
docs.text:
Person
  ↓
Agent
  ↓
Performs work repeatedly

Example:

  • Research agent
  • QA agent
  • Compliance agent

docs.title: Multi-Agent System
docs.text:
Person
  ↓
Orchestrator
  ↓
10 agents
  ↓
1000 tasks

This is closer to your AgentPlexus work.

docs.title: Platform
docs.text:
Person
  ↓
Platform
  ↓
Many builders
  ↓
Many products

This is what ProductBuildersHQ, AgentPlexus, and AcademyOS aspire to become.

docs.title: Product Builder Ladder
docs.text:
One possible progression is:

Stage Primary Output Learner Knowledge Practitioner Tasks Builder Products Leader Teams Teacher Builders Multiplier Systems Institution Builder Ecosystems

Notice the output changes.

docs.title: Builder
docs.text: Creates a SaaS application.

docs.title: Teacher
docs.text: Creates another Product Builder.

docs.title: Multiplier
docs.text:
Creates:

  • AI agents
  • Platforms
  • Standards
  • Templates
  • Workflows
  • Automation

that allow many Product Builders to operate.

docs.title: Why This Resonates with Your Work
docs.text:
Looking at your recent projects:

Project Category AgentPlexus Multiplier MultiSpec Multiplier ProductBuildersHQ Multiplier Capability-Stack-Spec Multiplier PRISM Multiplier KYA Profiles Multiplier TopologySpec Multiplier

These are not primarily products.

They are capability systems.

They help other people build products.

That's why "Multiplier" feels like a distinct stage from "Teacher."

docs.title: CPTO Perspective
docs.text:
This distinction becomes even clearer at the executive level.

A traditional leader might think:

How do I manage 100 engineers?

A multiplier thinks:

How do I create a platform where 100 Product Builders can each operate at 10× leverage?

The second question naturally leads to:

  • AI agents
  • Internal developer platforms
  • Reusable architectures
  • Standards
  • Automation
  • Training systems

In other words, the CPTO becomes a multiplier of multipliers.

docs.title: AI-Era Definition
docs.text:
A concise definition could be:

Multiplier: A practitioner who creates reusable systems—human, AI, automated, or organizational—that increase the capability of many others.

Examples include:

  • Mentor
  • Teacher
  • Author
  • Framework creator
  • AI tool builder
  • AI agent builder
  • Multi-agent orchestrator
  • Platform architect
  • Standards author
  • Community builder

So an AI agent/subagent builder is absolutely a multiplier, but the broader concept is:

Teacher multiplies through people.

Multiplier multiplies through people, AI, automation, platforms, and institutions.

That distinction is what makes the concept particularly relevant to Product Builders and AI-native organizations.

===

I think it is a material change, but not because AI suddenly invented multiplication.

The material change is that multiplication has become accessible to far more people.

docs.title: Before AI
docs.text: There were essentially three ways to scale your impact:

docs.title: 1. Teach People
docs.text:
Expert
→ Mentor
→ Team

Slow, expensive, and limited by human bandwidth.

docs.title: 2. Build Organizations
docs.text:
Expert
→ Manager
→ Director
→ VP

Scale came through hierarchy.

docs.title: 3. Build Platforms
docs.text:
Expert
→ Product
→ Platform
→ Ecosystem

Very powerful, but difficult and capital-intensive.

Historically, only a relatively small number of people reached this level.

docs.title: After AI
docs.text:
A single Product Builder can now create:

  • AI workflows
  • Agents
  • Multi-agent systems
  • Code generators
  • Evaluation frameworks
  • Design systems
  • Spec systems
  • Internal platforms

that immediately amplify the work of many others.

The cost of becoming a multiplier has dropped dramatically.

That is the real shift.

docs.title: Why This Matters for Product Builders
docs.text:
Traditional PM career ladders implicitly assumed:

IC PM
→ Senior PM
→ Director
→ VP
→ CPO

The path to more impact was usually:

Manage more people.

But Product Builders are taking a different path.

Builder
→ Tool Builder
→ Platform Builder
→ Multiplier

The path to more impact becomes:

Enable more capability.

That's a different optimization function.

docs.title: Why This Matters for AI-Native Organizations
docs.text:
Traditional organizations scale through headcount.

10 PMs
50 Engineers
10 QA
5 Designers

AI-native organizations increasingly scale through leverage.

10 Product Builders
+
AI agents
+
Automation
+
Platforms

This doesn't necessarily mean fewer humans forever.

It means each human owns a much larger capability envelope.

This aligns closely with your "responsibility collapse" idea.

docs.title: Product Builder vs Product Manager
docs.text: This is where the distinction becomes significant.

docs.title: Product Manager Era
docs.text:
Primary skill:

Alignment
Coordination
Prioritization

Success often depended on influencing specialists.

docs.title: Product Builder Era
docs.text:
Primary skill:

Creation
Orchestration
Multiplication

Success increasingly depends on:

  • Building prototypes
  • Shipping solutions
  • Directing AI systems
  • Creating reusable assets

The role shifts from coordinator toward creator.

docs.title: CPTO Implications
docs.text:
Historically, executives scaled through:

Manager
→ Managers
→ Directors
→ VPs

In an AI-native organization, a CPTO may increasingly scale through:

Platforms
Standards
AI systems
Automation
Product Builders

The question changes from:

How many people report into me?

to

How much capability can the organization generate per person?

That is a fundamentally different management question.

docs.title: Is This a New Learning Stage?
docs.text:
This is where I'd be careful.

I would not claim:

AI invented a new stage of human learning.

That's probably too strong.

People have been multipliers for centuries:

  • Teachers
  • Authors
  • Inventors
  • Framework creators
  • Platform builders

all multiplied capability.

What AI changes is:

The ease, speed, and scale at which an individual can become a multiplier.

That is a much more defensible claim.

docs.title: The ProductBuildersHQ Opportunity
docs.text:
This is why I think the strongest framing is not:

"We created a new learning theory."

Instead:

"We adapted classical mastery frameworks for the AI era."

The contribution is not replacing Bloom or Dreyfus.

The contribution is recognizing that AI dramatically increases the importance of the upper end of the ladder:

Learn
→ Apply
→ Create
→ Lead
→ Teach
→ Multiply

Historically, most professionals spent their careers in the first three stages.

AI-native Product Builders can reach the multiplication stage much earlier because they can build agents, workflows, standards, platforms, and automation without needing a large organization behind them.

That's why this matters to ProductBuildersHQ. The central thesis becomes:

AI does not merely make experts more productive. It enables experts to become multipliers much sooner in their careers.

That is a meaningful shift for Product Builders, Solo Founders, CPTOs, and AI-native organizations. It also connects directly to your two maturity models: one measures how individuals progress toward becoming multipliers, while the other measures how teams and organizations operationalize that multiplied capability.

===

Given where the discussion landed, I would optimize for these criteria:

  1. Maps cleanly to existing frameworks (Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship)
  2. Adds the AI-era concept of multiplication
  3. Works for ProductBuildersHQ, ProofMinds, AcademyOS, AgentPlexus
  4. Works for individuals, teams, and organizations
  5. Can become a certification framework
  6. Feels timeless, not AI-hype specific

docs.title: My Favorite: SCALE
docs.text: This is the strongest overall candidate.

docs.title: SCALE
docs.text:
Study Competence Autonomy Leadership Expansion

Stage Description Study Learn concepts Competence Apply skills Autonomy Operate independently Leadership Develop others Expansion Multiply capability

Why it works:

docs.title: Bloom
docs.text: SCALE Bloom Study Remember, Understand Competence Apply Autonomy Analyze, Evaluate Leadership Create Expansion Beyond Bloom

docs.title: Dreyfus
docs.text: SCALE Dreyfus Study Novice Competence Competent Autonomy Proficient Leadership Expert Expansion Master/Teacher

docs.title: AI Era Extension
docs.text:
Expansion means:

  • Teaching
  • Frameworks
  • Platforms
  • AI agents
  • AI workflows
  • Standards
  • Institutions

Exactly the area we've been discussing.

docs.title: Alternative: LEVER
docs.text: This may actually be the most elegant.

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Learn Execute Validate Enable Replicate

The entire theme is increasing leverage.

Stage Output Learn Knowledge Execute Results Validate Expertise Enable Other builders Replicate Systems and institutions

This directly aligns with:

Capability multiplication

which seems to be the real thesis.

docs.title: Alternative: ASCEND
docs.text: Most certification-friendly.

docs.title: ASCEND
docs.text:
Awareness Skill Competence Expertise Nurture Distribution

The last two stages are where the AI-era concepts appear.

Nurture:

  • Mentoring
  • Coaching
  • Teaching

Distribution:

  • Platforms
  • AI agents
  • Frameworks
  • Standards
  • Ecosystems

docs.title: Alternative: AMPLIFY
docs.text: This is probably the most AI-era-specific.

docs.title: AMPLIFY
docs.text:
Awareness Mastery Practice Leadership Influence Frameworks Yield

The problem is that it's harder to remember the individual stage meanings.

docs.title: What I'd Recommend
docs.text:
For ProductBuildersHQ, ProofMinds, and AcademyOS, I would narrow it to:

Rank Acronym Why #1 SCALE Most universal and maps well to existing frameworks #2 LEVER Best expression of capability multiplication #3 ASCEND Best for education and certification #4 AMPLIFY Most AI-era specific

Personally, I think LEVER may be the deepest conceptually.

The core insight from our discussion is not really learning.

It's:

How does a person increase their leverage over time?

That works for:

  • Product Builders
  • Engineers
  • Designers
  • Solo Founders
  • CPTOs
  • AI Agents
  • Teams
  • Organizations

A possible LEVER ladder could be:

L — Learn       (knowledge acquisition)
E — Execute     (apply skills)
V — Validate    (demonstrate expertise)
E — Enable      (develop people)
R — Replicate   (create systems that multiply capability)

And the final stage, Replicate, is where the AI-era extension lives:

Capability can now be replicated not only through teaching, but through AI agents, automation, platforms, standards, and institutions.

That's a genuinely modern addition while still remaining compatible with Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, and Apprenticeship models.

===

Yes, LEVER has the potential to cover Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, leadership development, Product Builder maturity, and AI-native organizations—but only if you position it correctly.

I would not position LEVER as:

A replacement for Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, or Apprenticeship.

I would position it as:

A meta-framework that integrates learning, mastery, leadership, and capability multiplication.

That's a much stronger claim.

docs.title: Why LEVER Works
docs.text:
Most existing frameworks focus on a specific dimension:

Framework Primary Focus Bloom Cognitive mastery Dreyfus Skill acquisition Shu-Ha-Ri Independence from rules Apprenticeship Responsibility progression Leadership Pipeline Organizational leadership Maxwell Leadership influence

LEVER can sit above them.

It answers:

How does a person increase leverage over time?

That is broader than learning alone.

docs.title: L — Learn
docs.text:
Acquire knowledge.

Questions:

  • What is it?
  • How does it work?

Maps to:

Framework Stage Bloom Remember, Understand Dreyfus Novice Shu-Ha-Ri Shu Apprenticeship Observe

Output:

Knowledge

docs.title: E — Execute
docs.text:
Apply knowledge.

Questions:

  • Can I do it?
  • Can I deliver results?

Maps to:

Framework Stage Bloom Apply Dreyfus Advanced Beginner / Competent Shu-Ha-Ri Shu Apprenticeship Assist / Perform

Output:

Results

docs.title: V — Validate
docs.text:
Develop judgment and expertise.

Questions:

  • Can I consistently solve difficult problems?
  • Can I make good decisions under uncertainty?

Maps to:

Framework Stage Bloom Analyze, Evaluate Dreyfus Proficient / Expert Shu-Ha-Ri Ha Apprenticeship Journeyman

Output:

Expertise

This is where many maturity models stop.

docs.title: E — Enable
docs.text:
Develop other people.

Questions:

  • Can I teach?
  • Can I mentor?
  • Can I build teams?

Maps to:

Framework Stage Apprenticeship Master Leadership Pipeline First-level Leader Maxwell People Development

Output:

Capability in Others

This is where learning transitions into leadership.

docs.title: R — Replicate
docs.text:
Create systems that scale capability.

Questions:

  • Can I create platforms?
  • Can I create frameworks?
  • Can I create AI agents?
  • Can I create institutions?

Output:

Capability Systems

Examples:

  • Standards
  • Frameworks
  • Design systems
  • Agent platforms
  • Training programs
  • Organizations

This is where Product Builder, Solo Founder, CPTO, and AI-native concepts emerge.

docs.title: Why LEVER Covers Leadership
docs.text:
This is the key insight.

Traditional learning frameworks focus on:

Learn
→ Master

Leadership frameworks focus on:

Master
→ Lead Others

LEVER includes both:

Learn
→ Execute
→ Validate
→ Enable
→ Replicate

The last two stages are leadership.

docs.title: Product Builder Mapping
docs.text: LEVER Product Builder Learn Learn product, technology, AI Execute Build prototypes Validate Ship production outcomes Enable Mentor builders Replicate Create platforms and ecosystems

docs.title: CPTO Mapping
docs.text:
LEVER CPTO Learn Domain knowledge Execute Deliver products Validate Strategic judgment Enable Build leaders Replicate Build organizational systems

Notice:

A CPTO's highest leverage is not building products.

It is creating:

  • Product Builders
  • Platforms
  • Standards
  • AI systems
  • Operating models

that build products.

docs.title: AI-Native Team Mapping
docs.text: This is where LEVER becomes interesting.

docs.title: Human Level
docs.text:
Learn
→ Execute
→ Validate
→ Enable
→ Replicate

docs.title: Team Level
docs.text:
Manual
→ Assisted
→ Automated
→ Agentic
→ Autonomous

The team maturity model becomes an implementation of LEVER.

docs.title: One Possible Refinement
docs.text:
The only weakness of LEVER is that Validate can sound a bit passive.

You may eventually prefer:

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Learn Execute Veteranize (or Verify) Enable Replicate

or

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Learn Execute Value Enable Replicate

But conceptually the stages are correct:

Knowledge
→ Results
→ Expertise
→ Leadership
→ Multiplication

docs.title: My Conclusion
docs.text:
Of all the candidates we've discussed, LEVER is the first one that naturally spans:

  • Bloom (learning)
  • Dreyfus (mastery)
  • Shu-Ha-Ri (independence)
  • Apprenticeship (responsibility)
  • Leadership Pipeline (people development)
  • Product Builder maturity
  • CPTO development
  • AI-native organizations
  • Capability multiplication

because it is not fundamentally a learning framework.

It is a leverage framework.

Learning becomes the first stage.

Leadership becomes the fourth stage.

AI-enabled multiplication becomes the fifth stage.

That makes it broad enough to serve as the foundational framework behind ProductBuildersHQ, AcademyOS, ProofMinds, PRISM capability ladders, and future certification programs.

===

Yes. In fact, I think you've identified the natural breakpoint in the model.

The first three stages are about personal capability.

The last two stages are about leveraged capability.

LEVER

L → Learn
E → Execute
V → Validate
------------------
E → Enable
R → Replicate

That divider is powerful.

docs.title: Learn
docs.text:
Acquire knowledge.

Output:

I know.

docs.title: Execute
docs.text:
Apply knowledge.

Output:

I can do.

docs.title: Validate
docs.text:
Demonstrate repeatable success.

Output:

I can succeed independently.

This is an important distinction.

Validate isn't merely expertise.

It's proven capability.

Examples:

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Ships products
  • Gains users
  • Drives adoption

docs.title: Engineer
docs.text:
• Delivers production systems
  • Operates them successfully

docs.title: Solo Founder
docs.text:
• Acquires customers
  • Generates revenue

docs.title: AI Practitioner
docs.text:
• Successfully deploys AI solutions

At this point, the person no longer needs supervision.

They have validated their capability in the real world.

This is where many frameworks stop.

docs.title: Leveraged Capability
docs.text:
After Validate, the question changes.

Before:

How capable am I?

After:

How much capability can I create?

docs.title: Enable
docs.text:
Develop other people.

Output:

I can make others successful.

Examples:

  • Mentor
  • Coach
  • Teacher
  • Team lead
  • Manager

This is classic leadership.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
Create systems that scale capability.

Output:

I can create success repeatedly.

Examples:

  • Frameworks
  • Standards
  • Playbooks
  • Platforms
  • AI agents
  • Training programs
  • Communities
  • Organizations

This is where multiplication occurs.

docs.title: Why This Matters for Product Builders
docs.text:
Traditional PM ladders often look like:

PM
→ Sr PM
→ Director
→ VP

which implicitly assumes:

More people reporting to you
=
More impact

LEVER suggests a different path.

Learn
→ Execute
→ Validate
→ Enable
→ Replicate

A Product Builder may reach Replicate without ever managing a large team.

Examples:

  • Creates a product framework.
  • Creates an AI workflow.
  • Creates a reusable platform.
  • Creates a standards body.
  • Creates a certification program.

Impact grows without headcount.

docs.title: Why This Matters for AI
docs.text:
Historically:

Enable

was the only scalable mechanism.

You trained people.

Today:

Enable
+
Replicate

can happen through:

  • Humans
  • Documentation
  • Agents
  • Workflows
  • Platforms
  • Standards

This is why AI changes the economics.

Not because learning changes.

Because replication becomes dramatically cheaper.

docs.title: A Useful Interpretation
docs.text:
You might define the stages as:

Stage Core Question Learn What do I know? Execute What can I do? Validate Can I succeed independently? Enable Can I make others successful? Replicate Can I make success repeatable?

I particularly like the last two because they distinguish:

docs.title: Enable
docs.text:
Person → Person

capability transfer.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
Person → System → Many People

capability multiplication.

docs.title: ProductBuildersHQ Interpretation
docs.text:
This could become the central thesis:

Learn
→ Execute
→ Validate

creates a successful practitioner.

Enable
→ Replicate

creates a multiplier.

And in the AI era, the highest leverage Product Builders, Solo Founders, CPTOs, and AI-native leaders increasingly spend their time in the last two stages:

  • Enabling people
  • Replicating capability through systems

rather than personally executing all the work themselves.

That distinction feels like a genuinely useful contribution because it connects classical learning theory with modern AI-era organizational design.

===

I actually think you're onto something important.

Validate is conceptually correct, but it has two weaknesses:

  1. It sounds passive.
  2. People immediately ask, "Validate what?"

In product, startup, and AI circles, "validation" usually means validating an idea, market, or hypothesis—not validating yourself as a practitioner.

The stage you're describing is really:

"I can independently create value in the real world."

That's stronger than validation.

docs.title: The Good Part About Value
docs.text:
Value captures the transition from:

Learning
→ Doing
→ Producing meaningful outcomes

A Product Builder isn't successful because they built something.

They're successful because they delivered value.

Likewise:

  • Engineer → delivers value
  • Product Manager → delivers value
  • Founder → delivers value
  • AI practitioner → delivers value

So philosophically, Value may be more accurate than Validate.

docs.title: The Problem with Value
docs.text:
As you observed:

Learn
Execute
Value
Enable
Replicate

The pattern breaks.

The others are verbs.

Value is usually a noun.

That makes the acronym feel slightly inconsistent.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
This is actually fine.

Many frameworks cheat a little.

For example:

SMART

  • Specific
  • Measurable
  • Achievable
  • Relevant
  • Time-bound

Not all are the same grammatical form.

The acronym still works.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
This preserves the verb.

But it becomes a little clunky.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
Then define Value as:

Demonstrates independent ability to create measurable outcomes.

This may be the cleanest.

docs.title: Another Interesting Option: Venture
docs.text: If you're willing to stretch the V:

docs.title: Venture
docs.text:
Meaning:

Take ownership and assume responsibility for outcomes.

This is very Product Builder and Solo Founder oriented.

Learn
→ Execute
→ Venture
→ Enable
→ Replicate

But it's less universal than Value.

docs.title: The Product Builder Lens
docs.text:
For ProductBuildersHQ, I actually like:

Learn
→ Execute
→ Value
→ Enable
→ Replicate

because it creates a very clear transition.

docs.title: Learn
docs.text: Acquire knowledge.

docs.title: Execute
docs.text: Apply knowledge.

docs.title: Value
docs.text: Create measurable outcomes.

docs.title: Enable
docs.text: Help others create outcomes.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
Create systems that repeatedly create outcomes.

Notice how the output evolves:

Stage Output Learn Knowledge Execute Work Value Outcomes Enable People Replicate Systems

That's a very elegant progression.

docs.title: Why Value May Be Better for the AI Era
docs.text:
The AI era is increasingly outcome-oriented.

Historically:

Learn
→ Execute
→ Expert

was enough.

Today nobody really cares whether you are an expert if you cannot produce outcomes.

AI compresses implementation effort.

The differentiator becomes:

Can you create value?

not

Can you demonstrate expertise?

That aligns closely with:

  • Product Builders
  • Solo Founders
  • CPTOs
  • AI-native organizations

because all are fundamentally judged by outcomes.

docs.title: My Recommendation
docs.text: I would seriously consider:

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Learn Execute Value Enable Replicate

And define it as:

Stage Question Learn What do I know? Execute What can I do? Value Can I independently create outcomes? Enable Can I make others successful? Replicate Can I make success repeatable at scale?

Of all the versions we've discussed, this is the one that feels most aligned with:

  • Product Builders
  • Solo Founders
  • CPTO development
  • AI-native organizations
  • Capability multiplication

because it puts outcomes and value creation at the center of the framework rather than expertise alone.

===

I would separate three concepts:

Concept Purpose Product Builder Maturity Model (PBMM) Individual Product Builder capability ASDM (Autonomous Software Delivery Model) Team/organization delivery automation maturity LEVER Universal human capability and leadership progression

The mistake would be making LEVER another maturity model.

LEVER is actually more fundamental.

docs.title: Think of LEVER as the Foundation
docs.text:
LEVER Framework
        │
        ├── Product Builder Maturity Model
        ├── ASDM
        ├── AcademyOS Certifications
        ├── ProofMinds Learning Paths
        └── Future Capability Models

LEVER becomes the theory.

PBMM and ASDM become implementations.

docs.title: Option 1: LEVER Framework
docs.text: This is probably the cleanest.

docs.title: LEVER Framework
docs.text:
Learn Execute Value Enable Replicate

Tagline:

A framework for progressing from individual capability to multiplied impact.

Advantages:

  • Short
  • Memorable
  • Applies to people, teams, and organizations
  • Works outside ProductBuildersHQ

docs.title: Option 2: LEVER Capability Framework
docs.text: This is probably my favorite.

docs.title: LEVER Capability Framework
docs.text:
Why?

Because the core concept isn't learning.

It's capability creation and multiplication.

Progression:

Knowledge
→ Action
→ Outcomes
→ People
→ Systems

This aligns with:

  • Product Builders
  • Leaders
  • Founders
  • AI-native organizations

docs.title: Option 3: LEVER Mastery Framework
docs.text: This positions it closer to Bloom and Dreyfus.

docs.title: LEVER Mastery Framework
docs.text:
Focus:

Learning
→ Mastery
→ Leadership
→ Multiplication

Good for AcademyOS.

Less good for ASDM.

docs.title: Option 4: LEVER Leadership Framework
docs.text:
I would avoid this.

Too narrow.

You want:

  • Learning
  • Product building
  • Leadership
  • AI leverage
  • Institution building

Leadership is only part of the story.

docs.title: Option 5: Capability Multiplication Framework
docs.text: This is the academically strongest name.

docs.title: Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)
docs.text:
LEVER becomes the mnemonic.

Capability Multiplication Framework

LEVER
  Learn
  Execute
  Value
  Enable
  Replicate

This has an interesting parallel to PRISM.

docs.title: PRISM
docs.text:
Measures:

Capability maturity.

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Measures:

Capability growth and multiplication.

docs.title: How It Fits ProductBuildersHQ
docs.text:
I would structure the site something like:

ProductBuildersHQ

├── LEVER Framework
│      Universal capability model
│
├── Product Builder Maturity Model
│      Individual capability assessment
│
├── ASDM
│      Software delivery automation maturity
│
├── Certifications
│      Mapped to LEVER stages
│
└── Playbooks

docs.title: The Strongest Positioning
docs.text:
If I were writing the website copy, I'd likely say:

LEVER (Learn, Execute, Value, Enable, Replicate) is a capability multiplication framework that synthesizes classical learning models such as Bloom's Taxonomy, the Dreyfus Model, Shu-Ha-Ri, and Apprenticeship for the AI era.

LEVER describes how individuals progress from acquiring knowledge to creating value, enabling others, and ultimately replicating capability through systems, automation, AI agents, platforms, and institutions.

Then:

  • PBMM measures the maturity of Product Builders.
  • ASDM measures the maturity of software delivery systems.
  • LEVER explains the progression underlying both.

That gives you a clean hierarchy:

LEVER
  ↓
PBMM
ASDM
AcademyOS
ProofMinds

which feels very similar to how PRISM sits above individual capability assessments and reference architectures.

===

Here's how I would summarize the naming options and where they fit within the ProductBuildersHQ / ProofMinds / AcademyOS ecosystem.

docs.title: Option 1: LEVER Framework
docs.text:
Name

LEVER Framework

Definition

  • Learn
  • Execute
  • Value
  • Enable
  • Replicate

Positioning

A universal progression model from learner to multiplier.

Pros

  • Simple and memorable
  • Easy to explain
  • Works for individuals, teams, leaders, founders, and AI practitioners
  • Acronym is naturally associated with leverage

Cons

  • "Framework" is somewhat generic
  • Doesn't immediately communicate learning or capability

Best Use

Public-facing framework name.

docs.title: Option 2: LEVER Capability Framework
docs.text:
Name

LEVER Capability Framework

Definition

  • Learn
  • Execute
  • Value
  • Enable
  • Replicate

Positioning

A framework describing how capability is acquired, demonstrated, scaled, and multiplied.

Pros

  • Closely aligned with PRISM
  • Broad enough for ProductBuildersHQ, ProofMinds, AcademyOS
  • Works for both human and AI systems
  • Strong connection to capability maturity models

Cons

  • Slightly longer name

Best Use

Master framework that sits above PBMM and ASDM.

docs.title: Option 3: LEVER Mastery Framework
docs.text:
Name

LEVER Mastery Framework

Positioning

A learning and mastery model.

Pros

  • Maps naturally to Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri
  • Excellent for education and certification programs
  • Familiar to learning professionals

Cons

  • Less suitable for organizations and AI-native teams
  • Doesn't emphasize multiplication as strongly

Best Use

AcademyOS curriculum and certification paths.

docs.title: Option 4: LEVER Leadership Framework
docs.text:
Name

LEVER Leadership Framework

Positioning

A leadership development model.

Pros

  • Strong fit for managers and executives
  • Clear connection to Enable and Replicate

Cons

  • Too narrow
  • Doesn't fit early learning stages well
  • Doesn't fit Product Builders or practitioners as naturally

Best Use

Potential future specialization, not the primary framework.

docs.title: Option 5: Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)
docs.text:
Name

Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)

with

LEVER = mnemonic

Example

Capability Multiplication Framework

LEVER
Learn
Execute
Value
Enable
Replicate

Positioning

A theory explaining how capability grows from individual knowledge to organizational and AI-enabled multiplication.

Pros

  • Most academically defensible
  • Distinctive intellectual property
  • Explicitly captures the AI-era insight
  • Works for humans, teams, AI agents, platforms, and institutions

Cons

  • Less catchy than LEVER
  • More abstract
  • Requires explanation

Best Use

White papers, research, methodology documents.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: I would use a two-level naming structure:

docs.title: Public Brand
docs.text:
LEVER Capability Framework

LEVER
Learn
Execute
Value
Enable
Replicate

This becomes the flagship model.

docs.title: Underlying Theory
docs.text:
Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)

The theory behind LEVER.

docs.title: Relationship to Existing Models
docs.text:
Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)
                │
                ▼
      LEVER Capability Framework
                │
    ┌───────────┼───────────┐
    ▼           ▼           ▼
   PBMM       ASDM      AcademyOS
(Product   (Autonomous  Certifications
 Builder    Software
 Maturity)  Delivery)

docs.title: Positioning Statement
docs.text:
A concise positioning statement could be:

LEVER (Learn, Execute, Value, Enable, Replicate) is a capability multiplication framework that synthesizes classical learning models such as Bloom's Taxonomy, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, and Apprenticeship for the AI era. It describes how individuals progress from acquiring knowledge to creating value, enabling others, and ultimately replicating capability through systems, automation, AI agents, platforms, and institutions.

Of all the options, LEVER Capability Framework strikes the best balance between memorability, applicability, and long-term extensibility across ProductBuildersHQ, ProofMinds, AcademyOS, PBMM, and ASDM.

===

Yes, I think lever-framework is a strong choice, but I would think about the long-term architecture of your ecosystem before locking it in.

docs.title: Option 1: lever-framework (Recommended)
docs.text:
GitHub slug

github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-framework

Pros:

  • Clear and descriptive
  • Immediately understandable

  • Similar to well-known repos like:

  • design-system-spec

  • capability-stack-spec
  • openapi-specification

  • Leaves room for:

  • PBMM

  • ASDM
  • Certifications
  • Assessments

Potential contents:

LEVER Framework
├── Overview
├── Theory
├── Mapping to Bloom
├── Mapping to Dreyfus
├── Mapping to Shu-Ha-Ri
├── Mapping to Apprenticeship
├── AI-era extensions
├── Product Builder interpretation
├── Leadership interpretation
├── Assessments
└── Certifications

This is my preferred option.

docs.title: Option 2: lever
docs.text:
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever

Pros:

  • Short
  • Memorable

Cons:

  • Ambiguous

  • Could later conflict with:

  • CLI

  • SDK
  • Agent
  • Assessment tool

I would reserve this for a future executable or platform.

docs.title: Option 3: capability-multiplication-framework
docs.text:
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/capability-multiplication-framework

Pros:

  • Most academically precise

Cons:

  • Too long
  • Hard to remember
  • Poor branding

Better as the theory described inside the repo.

docs.title: Option 4: lever-spec
docs.text:
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-spec

Only if you intend to formalize it as machine-readable.

For example:

level:
  id: value
  competencies:
  evidence:
  assessments:

similar to:

  • OpenAPI
  • OpenSLO
  • PBOM
  • capability-stack-spec

Today I don't think you're there yet.

docs.title: Option 5: lever-model
docs.text:
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-model

Not bad, but weaker than framework.

"Framework" implies:

  • Theory
  • Practices
  • Assessments
  • Certifications
  • Implementations

which is exactly what you're building.

docs.title: Ecosystem Architecture
docs.text:
Given your existing naming conventions, I would probably organize like this:

ProductBuildersHQ/
├── lever-framework
├── pbmm
├── asdm
├── product-builder-playbook
├── academyos
├── capability-stack-spec
├── prism
└── multispec

Where:

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text: Foundational theory

docs.title: PBMM
docs.text: Individual capability maturity

docs.title: ASDM
docs.text:
Team/organization delivery maturity

This mirrors how:

PRISM
    ↓
Capability Assessments

LEVER
    ↓
Human Capability Assessments

docs.title: One More Thought
docs.text:
Because you've been building a family of frameworks (PRISM, PBMM, ASDM, MultiSpec, Capability Stack Spec), I would strongly consider making LEVER the umbrella learning framework.

Then PBMM could literally state:

PBMM is built upon the LEVER Capability Framework.

and ASDM could state:

ASDM applies LEVER principles to software delivery systems and AI-native organizations.

That creates a coherent intellectual stack rather than a collection of unrelated maturity models.

So my recommendation would be:

docs.title: Repository
docs.text: github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-framework

docs.title: Formal Name
docs.text: LEVER Capability Framework

docs.title: Internal Theory
docs.text:
Capability Multiplication Framework (CMF)

This gives you a clean pattern similar to how PRISM is the memorable brand and the underlying methodology can evolve over time.

===

I agree. After everything we've discussed, I would not try to force all of your projects into the same category because they actually serve different layers.

A cleaner mental model is:

Layer Purpose Example Learning & Capability How people grow LEVER, PBMM Organizational Capability How organizations operate PRISM, Capability Stack Spec, ASDM Specification Systems How systems are defined MultiSpec, Capability Stack Spec JSON IR Execution Platforms How systems run AgentPlexus, AgentOS, CoreForge

So I would separate them.

docs.title: LEVER Capability Framework
docs.text:
The foundational theory.

Learn
Execute
Value
Enable
Replicate

Questions:

  • How do people learn?
  • How do they become Product Builders?
  • How do they become multipliers?

This is where Bloom, Dreyfus, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, leadership development, and AI-era multiplication are synthesized.

docs.title: PBMM
docs.text:
Product Builder Maturity Model

Measures:

Where is an individual on the Product Builder journey?

Example:

PBMM Level 1
PBMM Level 2
PBMM Level 3
...

LEVER explains why the stages exist.

PBMM measures them.

Relationship:

LEVER
  ↓
PBMM

Similar to:

Bloom
  ↓
Course Rubrics

docs.title: Organizational Capability
docs.text: This is a different axis.

docs.title: PRISM
docs.text:
Measures organizational outcomes and capabilities.

Examples:

  • Security
  • Engineering
  • Product
  • Support
  • Reliability

Questions:

How mature is this capability?

docs.title: Capability Stack Spec
docs.text:
This is not a learning framework.

It's a reference architecture / capability model.

Questions:

What capabilities should exist?

Examples:

Identity
Observability
SAST
DLP
CNAPP

It's essentially:

Capability Inventory
+
Reference Architecture

docs.title: ASDM
docs.text:
I actually think ASDM sits closer to PRISM than PBMM.

Because ASDM measures:

How autonomous is software delivery?

This is partly about people.

But mostly about systems.

Examples:

Manual
Assisted
Automated
Agentic
Autonomous

That is really organizational capability.

Not individual learning.

docs.title: MultiSpec
docs.text:
I would definitely keep MultiSpec separate.

MultiSpec is not:

  • Learning
  • Leadership
  • Capability maturity

It is:

Specification System

Questions:

How are requirements represented? How are multiple viewpoints reconciled? How are autonomous builders driven?

This is closer to:

  • OpenAPI
  • OpenSLO
  • Kubernetes CRDs

than to Bloom or LEVER.

docs.title: A Cleaner Structure
docs.text:
I would probably organize ProductBuildersHQ like this:

Human Capability
├── LEVER
└── PBMM

Organizational Capability
├── PRISM
├── ASDM
└── Capability Stack Spec

Specification Systems
├── MultiSpec
├── TopologySpec
├── Design System Spec
└── Future Specs

Execution Platforms
├── AgentPlexus
├── AgentOS
├── CoreForge
└── Future Platforms

docs.title: What This Means for LEVER
docs.text:
This actually strengthens the case for LEVER.

Because LEVER doesn't need to explain everything.

It only needs to explain:

How humans progress from learner to multiplier.

Which is exactly the gap that PBMM needs.

PBMM can then say:

PBMM is based on the LEVER Capability Framework.

while ASDM can remain independent.

docs.title: One Thing I'd Revisit
docs.text:
You mentioned:

ASDM is focused on individuals + organizations.

I would challenge that slightly.

The more we've discussed it, the more I think:

docs.title: PBMM
docs.text:
Measures:

Individual capability

docs.title: LEVER
docs.text:
Explains:

Human capability progression

docs.title: ASDM
docs.text:
Measures:

System capability progression

Even if humans participate.

The levels:

Manual
Assisted
Automated
Agentic
Autonomous

are describing the delivery system, not the human.

That distinction may become important later because it allows PBMM and ASDM to evolve independently.

A Level-5 Product Builder could operate in a Level-2 ASDM organization.

A Level-5 ASDM organization could contain many Level-2 Product Builders.

Those are orthogonal dimensions.

That's why I would make LEVER → PBMM one lineage, and PRISM → ASDM another lineage, rather than trying to place all four under a single hierarchy.

===

Yes, I would seriously consider using:

github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever

instead of:

github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-framework

for a few reasons.

docs.title: 1. It becomes a first-class concept
docs.text:
Compare:

github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/prism
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/pbmm
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/asdm
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever

versus

github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/prism
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/pbmm
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/asdm
github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever-framework

The first feels like a peer alongside your other flagship frameworks.

The second feels more like a document describing a framework.

docs.title: 2. You're building a body of knowledge, not just a framework
docs.text:
I suspect the repo will eventually contain:

LEVER
├── Theory
├── Framework
├── Assessments
├── Certifications
├── Mappings
├── Examples
├── Competencies
├── Badges
├── Learning Paths
└── PBMM integration

At that point "framework" is only one piece.

docs.title: 3. Consistent with successful framework repos
docs.text:
Many major frameworks use the concept name:

Examples:

  • openapi-specification is a specification (because specification is the product)
  • kubernetes
  • terraform
  • backstage
  • prism (if available)
  • solid (if it existed)

The strongest brands usually become the repo name.

docs.title: When I'd Choose lever-framework
docs.text:
Only if you anticipate creating:

lever
lever-cli
lever-sdk
lever-assessment
lever-certification

and need the namespace separation.

But I don't see evidence of that yet.

docs.title: Repository
docs.text: github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever

docs.title: Formal Name
docs.text: LEVER Capability Framework

docs.title: README Title
docs.text:
LEVER Capability Framework

Learn • Execute • Value • Enable • Replicate

docs.title: Positioning
docs.text: LEVER is a capability multiplication framework that synthesizes Bloom's Taxonomy, the Dreyfus Model, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, and modern AI-era leadership concepts into a unified progression from learner to multiplier.

docs.title: Long-Term Ecosystem
docs.text:
This feels clean:

ProductBuildersHQ/

lever
pbmm
asdm
prism
multispec
capability-stack-spec
structured-plan

Notice the pattern:

Repo Type lever Human capability framework pbmm Individual maturity model asdm Delivery maturity model prism Organizational capability model multispec Specification system capability-stack-spec Reference architecture model

Each repo name is the primary concept, not the implementation type.

That's generally the pattern that ages best as projects evolve.

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text: LEVER synthesizes insights from multiple classical learning and mastery frameworks, extending them for the AI era.

docs.title: Why Mappings Matter
docs.text:
By formally mapping LEVER to established frameworks, we:

  1. Build on proven theory — Leverage decades of research and practice
  2. Enable translation — Help practitioners familiar with other frameworks understand LEVER
  3. Identify gaps — Show where classical frameworks fall short
  4. Demonstrate rigor — Ground LEVER in academic foundations

docs.title: The Classical Frameworks
docs.text: Framework Era Primary Focus Bloom's Taxonomy 1956 Cognitive mastery levels Dreyfus Model 1980 Skill acquisition and independence Shu-Ha-Ri Traditional Relationship to rules and tradition Apprenticeship Medieval Responsibility and teaching progression

docs.title: Summary Mapping Table
docs.text:
LEVER Bloom Dreyfus Shu-Ha-Ri Apprenticeship Learn Remember, Understand Novice Shu Observe Execute Apply Adv. Beginner, Competent Shu Assist, Perform Value Analyze, Evaluate Proficient, Expert Ha Perform Enable Create — Ri Lead, Teach Replicate — — Ri Teach

See the full mapping table for detailed analysis.

docs.title: Where LEVER Extends
docs.text:
graph LR
    subgraph Classical["Classical Frameworks"]
        L1[Learning]
        L2[Mastery]
        L3[Teaching]
    end

    subgraph LEVER["LEVER Extension"]
        L4[Enable]
        L5[Replicate]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    style L4 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style L5 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333

Most classical frameworks implicitly assume:

  • Human effort is the primary constraint
  • Teaching is the main multiplication mechanism
  • Organizations are required for scale

LEVER explicitly addresses:

  • AI augmentation of capability
  • Systems (platforms, agents, automation) as multiplication mechanisms
  • Individuals reaching multiplication earlier in their careers

docs.title: Common Patterns
docs.text:
Despite their different origins, all frameworks share a similar progression:

  1. Dependence — Following rules, needing guidance
  2. Independence — Working autonomously, making decisions
  3. Multiplication — Scaling impact through others or systems

LEVER's contribution is making this explicit and extending it for the AI era.

docs.title: Framework Deep Dives
docs.text:
• Bloom's Taxonomy — Cognitive learning objectives
  • Dreyfus Model — Skill acquisition stages
  • Shu-Ha-Ri — Japanese mastery concept
  • Apprenticeship — Traditional craft progression
  • Mapping Table — Complete cross-reference

docs.title: Apprenticeship Model
docs.text:
Origin: Medieval guild system, formalized craft traditions

Primary Focus: Responsibility and teaching progression

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Apprenticeship Model is one of the oldest learning frameworks, originating in medieval trade guilds. It describes progression from observation through mastery to teaching, emphasizing hands-on learning under expert guidance.

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text: Stage Description Observe Watch experts perform the craft Assist Help experts with supervised tasks Perform Work independently on full tasks Lead Guide others, take responsibility for outcomes Teach Train apprentices, preserve and advance the craft

docs.title: Mapping to LEVER
docs.text: LEVER Apprenticeship Learn Observe Execute Assist, Perform Value Perform (mastery) Enable Lead, Teach Replicate Teach (at scale)

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
Learn ↔ Observe

Apprentices begin by watching masters work. They learn vocabulary, patterns, and techniques through observation before attempting work themselves.

Execute ↔ Assist, Perform

Execute spans assisting (working with guidance) to performing (working independently on defined tasks). This maps well to LEVER's Execute stage.

Value ↔ Perform (mastery)

At full Perform, the journeyman can create value independently. They're trusted with complete projects and client work.

Enable ↔ Lead, Teach

Leading and teaching others maps directly to LEVER's Enable. Masters develop the next generation of practitioners.

Replicate ↔ Teach (at scale)

Master teachers who create guilds, schools, or lasting methodologies are replicating at scale. However, traditional apprenticeship focused on human-to-human transfer, not systems.

docs.title: The Traditional Progression
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Apprentice] --> J[Journeyman]
    J --> M[Master]
    M --> GM[Guild Master]

    subgraph Learning["Learning Phase"]
        A
    end

    subgraph Working["Working Phase"]
        J
    end

    subgraph Teaching["Teaching Phase"]
        M
        GM
    end

docs.title: Apprentice
docs.text:
• Bound to a master for learning
  • Performs basic tasks
  • Limited autonomy
  • Typically 3-7 years

docs.title: Journeyman
docs.text:
• Completed apprenticeship
  • Can work independently
  • May travel to learn from multiple masters
  • Typically "day worker" (journée)

docs.title: Master
docs.text:
• Demonstrated masterwork
  • Can take apprentices
  • Full guild membership
  • Creates and teaches

docs.title: Strengths of Apprenticeship
docs.text:
• Time-tested over centuries
  • Emphasizes hands-on learning
  • Explicitly includes teaching
  • Clear progression with milestones
  • Community and tradition

docs.title: Limitations for LEVER's Purpose
docs.text:
• Human-to-human focus limits scale
  • Designed for physical crafts
  • No concept of systems or platforms
  • Slow progression (years per stage)

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Apprenticeship is the closest classical model to LEVER's full progression—it explicitly includes teaching. But it was designed when capability could only multiply through human relationships. LEVER extends this for the AI era.

docs.title: Modern Apprenticeship
docs.text: Traditional Modern Physical craft Knowledge work Single master Multiple mentors Guild certification Industry credentials Local community Global networks Years of training Accelerated learning

docs.title: The AI-Era Extension
docs.text:
Traditional apprenticeship:

Master → Apprentice → Master → Apprentice

AI-era replication:

Master → Platform/Agent → Many Practitioners

LEVER's Replicate stage captures this new multiplication path that wasn't available to medieval craftsmen.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Lave, J. & Wenger, E. (1991). Situated Learning: Legitimate Peripheral Participation. Cambridge University Press.

docs.title: Bloom's Taxonomy
docs.text:
Origin: Benjamin Bloom, 1956 (revised 2001)

Primary Focus: Cognitive mastery levels

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Bloom's Taxonomy is a hierarchical model of cognitive learning objectives widely used in education. It describes how learners progress from basic recall to complex creation.

docs.title: The Six Levels
docs.text: Level Description Example Remember Recall facts and basic concepts Define Kubernetes Understand Explain ideas or concepts Explain how pods work Apply Use information in new situations Deploy an application Analyze Draw connections among ideas Compare deployment strategies Evaluate Justify a decision or course of action Choose between EKS and GKE Create Produce new or original work Design a new platform architecture

docs.title: Mapping to LEVER
docs.text: LEVER Bloom Learn Remember, Understand Execute Apply Value Analyze, Evaluate Enable Create Replicate Beyond Bloom

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
Learn ↔ Remember, Understand

Both focus on knowledge acquisition. Bloom's Remember and Understand correspond to building foundational knowledge in LEVER's Learn stage.

Execute ↔ Apply

Direct correspondence. Both describe using learned knowledge to accomplish tasks.

Value ↔ Analyze, Evaluate

Creating independent value requires analysis and judgment—Bloom's higher cognitive levels.

Enable ↔ Create

Enabling others often involves creating new approaches, training materials, and systems. Bloom's Create aligns with the creative aspect of enablement.

Replicate ↔ Beyond Bloom

Bloom's Taxonomy focuses on individual cognitive mastery. It doesn't address multiplying capability through systems, platforms, or institutions. This is where LEVER extends beyond Bloom.

docs.title: Strengths of Bloom
docs.text:
• Well-established in education
  • Clear cognitive hierarchy
  • Widely understood vocabulary
  • Excellent for curriculum design

docs.title: Limitations for LEVER's Purpose
docs.text:
• Focuses on individual cognition, not leadership
  • Doesn't address multiplication or systems
  • No stages for teaching or developing others
  • Designed for educational settings, not professional development

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Bloom describes what you can do cognitively. LEVER describes how that cognition translates to capability and ultimately to multiplication.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Anderson, L.W. & Krathwohl, D.R. (2001). A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing: A Revision of Bloom's Taxonomy of Educational Objectives.

docs.title: Dreyfus Model
docs.text:
Origin: Stuart and Hubert Dreyfus, 1980

Primary Focus: Skill acquisition and independence

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Dreyfus Model of Skill Acquisition describes how students acquire skills through formal instruction and practice. Originally developed to study pilot training, it's widely used in software engineering, medicine, and professional development.

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text: Stage Description Characteristic Novice Follows rules exactly "Tell me the steps" Advanced Beginner Recognizes patterns from experience "I've seen this before" Competent Plans and prioritizes "I can manage this" Proficient Sees situations holistically "I know what matters here" Expert Operates intuitively "I immediately see what's wrong"

docs.title: Mapping to LEVER
docs.text: LEVER Dreyfus Learn Novice Execute Advanced Beginner, Competent Value Proficient, Expert Enable — (not covered) Replicate — (not covered)

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
Learn ↔ Novice

Novices are learning rules and fundamentals. They need explicit instructions and follow procedures exactly.

Execute ↔ Advanced Beginner, Competent

Execute spans pattern recognition (Advanced Beginner) to planning and prioritization (Competent). Both stages involve applying knowledge with varying degrees of guidance.

Value ↔ Proficient, Expert

Proficient practitioners see situations holistically. Experts operate intuitively. Both can create value independently—the hallmark of LEVER's Value stage.

Enable ↔ Not Covered

The original Dreyfus model stops at Expert. Some organizations add "Master" as an unofficial extension for those who develop others.

Replicate ↔ Not Covered

Dreyfus doesn't address system creation or capability multiplication. Some extensions add "Teacher" but this still focuses on human-to-human transfer.

docs.title: The Dreyfus Progression
docs.text:
graph LR
    N[Novice] --> AB[Adv. Beginner]
    AB --> C[Competent]
    C --> P[Proficient]
    P --> E[Expert]

    style N fill:#ffe0e0
    style AB fill:#ffe0e0
    style C fill:#fff0e0
    style P fill:#e0ffe0
    style E fill:#e0ffe0

Key transitions:

  • Novice → Advanced Beginner: Rules become patterns
  • Advanced Beginner → Competent: Patterns become plans
  • Competent → Proficient: Plans become holistic understanding
  • Proficient → Expert: Analysis becomes intuition

docs.title: Strengths of Dreyfus
docs.text:
• Research-backed progression
  • Clear stages for skill development
  • Widely adopted in professional contexts
  • Describes the path from rule-following to intuition

docs.title: Limitations for LEVER's Purpose
docs.text:
• Stops at Expert (individual mastery)
  • Doesn't address leadership or teaching
  • No stages for capability multiplication
  • Focused on skill acquisition, not impact

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Dreyfus describes how expertise develops. LEVER asks: what happens after expertise—and how does that expertise multiply?

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Dreyfus, S.E. & Dreyfus, H.L. (1980). A Five-Stage Model of the Mental Activities Involved in Directed Skill Acquisition. University of California, Berkeley.

docs.title: Mapping Table
docs.text: This page provides the complete cross-reference between LEVER stages and classical frameworks.

docs.title: Full Mapping Matrix
docs.text:

docs.text: Framework Stage(s) Notes Bloom Analyze, Evaluate Creating value requires analysis and judgment Dreyfus Proficient, Expert Holistic understanding and intuition Shu-Ha-Ri Ha Breaking from tradition, understanding context Apprenticeship Perform Full independent performance with proven results Conscious Competence UC Automatic, reliable performance

    end
