docs.title: What Classical Frameworks Cover Well
docs.text:
• Knowledge acquisition (Learn)
  • Skill application (Execute)
  • Expertise development (Value)

docs.title: Where Classical Frameworks Fall Short
docs.text:
• Developing others (Enable) — Partially covered by Apprenticeship
  • System creation (Replicate) — Not addressed by any classical framework

docs.title: The LEVER Extension
docs.text:
LEVER's contribution is making explicit what classical frameworks leave implicit or ignore:

  1. Enable as a distinct stage — Not just "what experts eventually do" but a specific capability
  2. Replicate as AI-era multiplication — Systems, platforms, and agents as multiplication mechanisms
  3. Personal vs Leveraged — Clear distinction between individual and multiplied capability

docs.title: For Curriculum Design
docs.text: Use Bloom and Dreyfus for Learn/Execute/Value stages. Use LEVER for Enable/Replicate.

docs.title: For Career Ladders
docs.text: Classical frameworks inform IC progression. LEVER extends to leadership and multiplication.

docs.title: For Assessment
docs.text:
Different frameworks may be appropriate for different stages:

Stage Best Framework for Assessment Learn Bloom (cognitive levels) Execute Dreyfus (skill independence) Value Dreyfus + outcome metrics Enable Leadership frameworks Replicate Impact metrics

docs.title: Shu-Ha-Ri
docs.text:
Origin: Japanese martial arts tradition, popularized in software by Alistair Cockburn

Primary Focus: Relationship to rules and tradition

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Shu-Ha-Ri (守破離) is a Japanese concept describing the stages of learning to mastery. It originated in martial arts and tea ceremony but has been widely adopted in software craftsmanship and Agile communities.

docs.title: The Three Stages
docs.text: Stage Japanese Meaning Description Shu 守 Protect/Obey Follow the rules exactly, learn the forms Ha 破 Detach/Break Break from tradition, understand when rules bend Ri 離 Leave/Transcend Create your own approach, rules become instinct

docs.title: Mapping to LEVER
docs.text: LEVER Shu-Ha-Ri Learn Shu Execute Shu Value Ha Enable Ri Replicate Ri

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
Learn, Execute ↔ Shu

In Shu, the practitioner follows the master's teachings exactly. They don't question or modify—they absorb. This maps to both Learn (absorbing knowledge) and Execute (applying it faithfully).

Value ↔ Ha

Ha is where the practitioner begins to understand why the rules exist. They can break from tradition when context demands. This corresponds to LEVER's Value—independent judgment and outcome creation.

Enable, Replicate ↔ Ri

At Ri, the practitioner transcends the rules. They create their own style and often become teachers who establish new schools. This maps to both Enable (teaching) and Replicate (creating new approaches).

docs.title: The Shu-Ha-Ri Journey
docs.text:
graph LR
    S[Shu<br/>守<br/>Follow] --> H[Ha<br/>破<br/>Break]
    H --> R[Ri<br/>離<br/>Transcend]

    style S fill:#e0e0ff
    style H fill:#ffe0e0
    style R fill:#e0ffe0

docs.title: Shu (守) — Protect
docs.text:
• Follow the teacher exactly
  • Don't question the forms
  • Practice until techniques become automatic
  • Trust the tradition

docs.title: Ha (破) — Break
docs.text:
• Understand the principles behind the forms
  • Question when rules apply
  • Adapt techniques to new situations
  • Develop personal style within tradition

docs.title: Ri (離) — Leave
docs.text:
• Transcend the forms entirely
  • Create new approaches
  • Rules become instinct
  • May establish new schools

docs.title: Strengths of Shu-Ha-Ri
docs.text:
• Elegant simplicity
  • Emphasizes respect for tradition before innovation
  • Captures the journey from imitation to creation
  • Popular in craftsmanship-oriented communities

docs.title: Limitations for LEVER's Purpose
docs.text:
• Very high-level (only three stages)
  • Doesn't distinguish learning from execution
  • Multiplication through systems not addressed
  • Originally designed for master-apprentice relationships

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Shu-Ha-Ri describes the relationship to rules. LEVER asks: what do you produce at each stage, and how does impact multiply?

docs.title: In Software
docs.text:
Alistair Cockburn brought Shu-Ha-Ri to software development:

Stage Software Example Shu Follow Scrum by the book Ha Adapt Scrum to team context Ri Create new methodology

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Cockburn, A. (2006). Agile Software Development: The Cooperative Game (2nd ed.). Addison-Wesley.

docs.title: Domain Profiles
docs.text: LEVER is a universal framework. Profiles adapt it to specific domains, defining what each stage means in context.

docs.title: What is a Profile?
docs.text:
A Profile provides domain-specific interpretation of LEVER stages:

  • Stage titles — Domain-appropriate names (e.g., "Builder" instead of "Value")
  • Descriptions — What each stage means in this domain
  • Competencies — Specific capabilities at each stage
  • Evidence — Observable indicators of stage attainment
  • Examples — Concrete illustrations

docs.title: Reference Profiles
docs.text:
LEVER includes two reference profiles:

Profile Domain Description Product Builder Product End-to-end product ownership Software Engineer Engineering Technical delivery

docs.title: Profile Structure
docs.text:
profile:
  id: product-builder
  name: Product Builder
  domain: product
  stage_interpretations:
    - stage_id: learn
      title: Learner
      description: Acquiring product, technology, and business knowledge
      evidence:
        - Completes relevant courses
        - Studies successful products
      examples:
        - Takes product management courses
        - Learns AI coding assistants

docs.title: Creating Custom Profiles
docs.text: Organizations can create profiles for their specific roles:

docs.title: 1. Define the Domain
docs.text:
What area does this profile cover?

  • Product
  • Engineering
  • Design
  • Leadership
  • Sales
  • etc.

docs.title: 2. Interpret Each Stage
docs.text:
For each LEVER stage, define:

Element Question Title What do we call this level? Description What does this stage mean here? Evidence How do we know someone is at this stage? Examples What does this look like in practice?

docs.title: 3. Define Competencies (Optional)
docs.text:
Specific capabilities with observable indicators:

competencies:
  - id: customer-discovery
    name: Customer Discovery
    description: Ability to identify and validate customer needs
    indicators:
      - Conducts effective user interviews
      - Synthesizes feedback into insights
      - Identifies patterns across conversations

docs.title: Engineering Domains
docs.text:
• Software Engineer — General software development
  • Platform Engineer — Internal developer platforms
  • ML Engineer — Machine learning systems
  • Security Engineer — Security and compliance

docs.title: Product Domains
docs.text:
• Product Builder — Full-stack product ownership
  • Product Manager — Traditional PM focus
  • Product Designer — Design-focused product role

docs.title: Leadership Domains
docs.text:
• Engineering Manager — Technical leadership
  • Product Leader — Product organization leadership
  • Executive — C-level progression

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
Profiles provide criteria for evaluating where someone is on the LEVER progression:

  1. Review evidence requirements for each stage
  2. Evaluate demonstrated competencies
  3. Identify current stage and growth areas

docs.title: Development Planning
docs.text:
Use profiles to create growth plans:

  1. Identify current stage
  2. Review requirements for next stage
  3. Create development activities targeting gaps

docs.title: Career Ladders
docs.text:
Profiles can inform career ladder design:

LEVER Stage Example Title Learn Associate Engineer Execute Engineer Value Senior Engineer Enable Staff Engineer Replicate Principal Engineer

docs.title: Relationship to PBMM and ASDM
docs.text:
PBMM (Product Builder Maturity Model) is essentially the Product Builder profile with detailed assessment criteria.

ASDM (Autonomous Software Delivery Model) applies LEVER concepts to team capability rather than individual profiles.

graph TD
    LEVER[LEVER Framework] --> Profiles[Individual Profiles]
    LEVER --> ASDM[Team Maturity]

    Profiles --> PB[Product Builder]
    Profiles --> SE[Software Engineer]
    Profiles --> Custom[Custom Profiles]

    PB --> PBMM[PBMM Assessment]

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Product Builder Profile
  • Software Engineer Profile
  • Usage: Go Library — Creating profiles programmatically

docs.title: Software Engineer Profile
docs.text:
Domain: Engineering

Description: LEVER interpretation for software engineers building production systems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Software Engineer profile maps LEVER stages to traditional engineering career progression, from learning fundamentals to creating platforms that multiply engineering capability.

docs.title: Learn → Junior Engineer
docs.text:
Description: Learning fundamentals of software development.

Evidence:

  • Completes coding tutorials and courses
  • Understands basic CS concepts
  • Learns team tools and practices
  • Studies documentation and codebases

Examples:

  • Completes language tutorials
  • Sets up development environment
  • Reads team coding standards
  • Shadows senior engineers

docs.title: Execute → Engineer
docs.text:
Description: Implementing features and fixing bugs with guidance.

Evidence:

  • Completes assigned tasks
  • Writes code that passes review
  • Debugs issues with support
  • Contributes to team projects

Examples:

  • Fixes bugs from issue tracker
  • Implements well-specified features
  • Writes unit tests
  • Participates in code reviews

docs.title: Value → Senior Engineer
docs.text:
Description: Independently delivering significant technical work.

Evidence:

  • Owns technical decisions
  • Delivers complex features end-to-end
  • Mentors junior engineers informally
  • Trusted to work independently

Examples:

  • Designs and implements new services
  • Leads incident response
  • Makes architectural decisions
  • Improves team processes

docs.title: Enable → Staff Engineer
docs.text:
Description: Elevating team capability and technical direction.

Evidence:

  • Sets technical direction for the team
  • Mentors multiple engineers
  • Leads technical initiatives
  • Influences beyond immediate team

Examples:

  • Defines team architecture
  • Runs technical training sessions
  • Leads cross-team projects
  • Creates engineering standards

docs.title: Replicate → Principal/Architect
docs.text:
Description: Creating systems that multiply engineering capability.

Evidence:

  • Creates reusable platforms and frameworks
  • Defines engineering standards organization-wide
  • Builds tools used across many teams
  • Establishes technical communities

Examples:

  • Builds internal developer platform
  • Creates company-wide coding standards
  • Develops shared libraries and tools
  • Establishes architecture review process

docs.title: Career Progression
docs.text: Stage Title Scope Learn Junior Engineer Task Execute Engineer Feature Value Senior Engineer Project Enable Staff Engineer Team Replicate Principal Engineer Organization

docs.title: Coding
docs.text:
• Language proficiency
  • Code quality
  • Testing practices
  • Debugging skills

docs.title: System Design
docs.text:
• Architecture patterns
  • Scalability considerations
  • Security awareness
  • Performance optimization

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
• Deployment practices
  • Monitoring and observability
  • Incident response
  • Production support

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text:
• Code review
  • Documentation
  • Knowledge sharing
  • Cross-team work

docs.title: Learn → Execute
docs.text:
• [ ] Demonstrates language proficiency
  • [ ] Understands team codebase
  • [ ] Can complete tasks with guidance
  • [ ] Writes acceptable code

docs.title: Execute → Value
docs.text:
• [ ] Delivers features independently
  • [ ] Makes sound technical decisions
  • [ ] Handles ambiguous requirements
  • [ ] Trusted with production systems

docs.title: Value → Enable
docs.text:
• [ ] Consistent technical excellence
  • [ ] Actively develops others
  • [ ] Leads technical initiatives
  • [ ] Influences team direction

docs.title: Enable → Replicate
docs.text:
• [ ] Developed multiple senior engineers
  • [ ] Created lasting technical improvements
  • [ ] Sees systemic opportunities
  • [ ] Can impact organization scale

docs.title: The IC vs Management Split
docs.text:
LEVER's Enable and Replicate stages can be reached on both IC and management tracks:

Stage IC Path Management Path Enable Staff Engineer Engineering Manager Replicate Principal Engineer Director/VP

Both paths involve multiplication—through technical systems (IC) or through organizational systems (management).

docs.title: AI-Era Considerations
docs.text:
In the AI era, engineers can leverage AI to:

  • Accelerate Learn → Execute with AI coding assistants
  • Reach Value faster by automating routine tasks
  • Enable at scale through AI-powered tools
  • Replicate through agents that multiply capability

The core progression remains, but AI compression the timeline and amplifies impact at each stage.

docs.title: Product Builder Profile
docs.text:
Domain: Product

Description: LEVER interpretation for Product Builders—individuals who own end-to-end product outcomes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Product Builder role represents the evolution of product management in the AI era. Unlike traditional PMs who coordinate specialists, Product Builders own vision, design, development, and delivery.

Product Builder Definition

A Product Builder is a mini solo founder inside an enterprise—owning outcomes, not just requirements.

docs.title: Learn → Learner
docs.text:
Description: Acquiring product, technology, and business knowledge.

Evidence:

  • Completes relevant courses and certifications
  • Studies successful products and case studies
  • Learns technical fundamentals (coding, AI, tools)
  • Understands business models and metrics

Examples:

  • Takes product management courses
  • Learns to use AI coding assistants
  • Studies customer discovery techniques
  • Completes technical bootcamps

docs.title: Execute → Practitioner
docs.text:
Description: Building prototypes and MVPs with guidance.

Evidence:

  • Creates functional prototypes
  • Uses AI tools to accelerate development
  • Completes features with supervision
  • Participates in product delivery

Examples:

  • Builds an internal tool with AI assistance
  • Ships a feature to production
  • Conducts user interviews
  • Creates product documentation

docs.title: Value → Builder
docs.text:
Description: Independently delivering products that create business value.

Evidence:

  • Ships products without supervision
  • Drives measurable business outcomes
  • Makes sound product decisions
  • Owns the full product lifecycle

Examples:

  • Launches a product that acquires users
  • Improves key metrics through product changes
  • Manages a product roadmap independently
  • Handles stakeholder communication autonomously

docs.title: Enable → Leader
docs.text:
Description: Developing other Product Builders.

Evidence:

  • Mentors junior builders
  • Creates learning opportunities
  • Builds high-performing teams
  • Establishes product practices

Examples:

  • Mentors a practitioner to builder level
  • Runs product craft sessions
  • Defines team product processes
  • Creates onboarding programs

docs.title: Replicate → Multiplier
docs.text:
Description: Creating systems that scale product capability.

Evidence:

  • Creates reusable frameworks and playbooks
  • Builds AI-powered product tools
  • Defines organizational standards
  • Establishes product communities

Examples:

  • Creates a product requirements AI agent
  • Writes a product playbook adopted by teams
  • Establishes a product guild or community
  • Builds a platform used by other builders

docs.title: Relationship to PBMM
docs.text:
This profile is the foundation for the Product Builder Maturity Model (PBMM), which provides detailed assessment criteria and benchmarks for each stage.

See PBMM on ProductBuildersHQ for the full maturity model.

docs.title: LEVER Ecosystem
docs.text: LEVER is designed to integrate with other capability and maturity frameworks.

docs.title: PBMM (Product Builder Maturity Model)
docs.text:

LEVER Stage PBMM Level Focus Learn Associate Learning fundamentals Execute PB I Shipping with guidance Value PB II Independent delivery Enable Senior PB Team development Replicate Principal PB System creation

docs.title: ASDM (Autonomous Software Delivery Model)
docs.text:
Autonomous Software Delivery Model applies LEVER concepts to team capability rather than individual progression.

LEVER Stage ASDM Level Team Capability Learn Level 1 Learning processes Execute Level 2 Following playbooks Value Level 3 Autonomous delivery Enable Level 4 Cross-team enablement Replicate Level 5 Platform/system creation

ASDM provides:

  • Capability benchmarks
  • Transformation roadmaps
  • Metrics and measurement

Learn more: Product Builders HQ

docs.text:

LEVER + AI acceleration:

docs.title: Profile Extension
docs.text:
Organizations can create domain-specific profiles that:

  1. Map to LEVER stages — Use the 5-stage progression
  2. Add domain competencies — Define specific capabilities
  3. Integrate with tools — Connect to existing HR/development systems
  4. Measure progress — Use LEVER questions as assessment criteria

docs.title: Maturity Model Pattern
docs.text:
LEVER stages map naturally to maturity levels:

// Map LEVER to your maturity model
var maturityMapping = map[lever.StageID]int{
    lever.StageLearn:     1,
    lever.StageExecute:   2,
    lever.StageValue:     3,
    lever.StageEnable:    4,
    lever.StageReplicate: 5,
}

docs.title: For Organizations
docs.text:
1. Start with reference profiles — Use Product Builder or Engineer profiles
  2. Customize for your domain — Create profiles that match your roles
  3. Integrate with career ladders — Map LEVER stages to your levels

docs.title: JSON Schema Reference
docs.text: LEVER provides JSON Schemas for validation and tooling integration.

docs.title: Available Schemas
docs.text: Schema Description URL lever.schema.json Complete LEVER specification schema/lever.schema.json profile.schema.json Domain profile definition schema/profile.schema.json

docs.title: Generating Schemas
docs.text:
Schemas are generated from Go types:

# Generate spec schema
lever schema > schema/lever.schema.json

# Generate profile schema
lever profile-schema > schema/profile.schema.json

docs.title: lever.schema.json
docs.text:
The main specification schema defines:

{
  "$id": "https://productbuildershq.com/schemas/lever.schema.json",
  "title": "LEVER Capability Framework",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "version": { "type": "string" },
    "name": { "type": "string" },
    "description": { "type": "string" },
    "stages": { "type": "array", "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/Stage" } },
    "frameworks": { "type": "array", "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/Framework" } },
    "mappings": { "type": "array", "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/FrameworkMappings" } },
    "mapping_table": { "$ref": "#/$defs/MappingTable" },
    "reference_profiles": { "type": "array", "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/Profile" } }
  }
}

docs.title: Stage
docs.text:
{
  "$defs": {
    "Stage": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "id": { "$ref": "#/$defs/StageID" },
        "name": { "type": "string" },
        "order": { "type": "integer" },
        "question": { "type": "string" },
        "output": { "type": "string" },
        "description": { "type": "string" },
        "characteristics": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } }
      },
      "required": ["id", "name", "order", "question", "output", "description"]
    }
  }
}

  "$defs": {

docs.title: profile.schema.json
docs.text:
The profile schema for domain-specific interpretations:

{
  "$id": "https://productbuildershq.com/schemas/profile.schema.json",
  "title": "LEVER Profile",

  "properties": {

docs.title: Using ajv (Node.js)
docs.text:
npm install -g ajv-cli

# Validate a spec
ajv validate -s schema/lever.schema.json -d my-spec.json

# Validate a profile
ajv validate -s schema/profile.schema.json -d my-profile.json

docs.title: Using Go
docs.text:
import (
    "github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever/schema"
    "github.com/xeipuuv/gojsonschema"
)

func validateProfile(profileJSON []byte) error {
    schemaLoader := gojsonschema.NewStringLoader(string(schema.ProfileSchema))
    docLoader := gojsonschema.NewBytesLoader(profileJSON)

    result, err := gojsonschema.Validate(schemaLoader, docLoader)
    if err != nil {
        return err
    }

    if !result.Valid() {
        for _, err := range result.Errors() {
            fmt.Printf("- %s\n", err)
        }
        return errors.New("validation failed")
    }

    return nil
}

docs.title: v0.1.0
docs.text:
Release Date: 2026-06-01

Commit: 3cb7165

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• LEVER Capability Framework — A five-stage progression model synthesizing classical learning frameworks for the AI era
  • Go-first type system — JSON Schema generation and TypeScript/Zod exports from Go structs
  • Classical framework mappings — Bidirectional mappings to 5 frameworks: Bloom's Taxonomy, Dreyfus Model, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship, Conscious Competence

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text:
LEVER defines a universal capability progression:

Stage Question Output Learn Can I follow instructions? Knowledge Execute Can I complete tasks? Work Value Can I independently create outcomes? Outcomes Enable Can I develop others? People Replicate Can I multiply my impact? Systems

The first three stages (L-E-V) represent personal capability. The last two (E-R) represent leveraged capability — multiplying impact through others and systems.

docs.title: Core Framework
docs.text:
• Core LEVER stage types with questions, outputs, and characteristics
  • Stage progression split: personal vs leveraged
  • Output progression: Knowledge → Work → Outcomes → People → Systems

docs.title: Classical Framework Mappings
docs.text:
• Bloom's Taxonomy — Cognitive complexity levels
  • Dreyfus Model — Skill acquisition stages
  • Shu-Ha-Ri — Japanese mastery progression
  • Apprenticeship Model — Traditional craft learning
  • Conscious Competence — Awareness of ability

docs.title: Domain Profiles
docs.text:
Two reference profiles for domain-specific interpretation:

  • Product Builder — For end-to-end product ownership roles
  • Software Engineer — For technical delivery roles

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text: LEVER defines five progressive stages of capability development, from initial learning to capability multiplication.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
    L[Learn] --> E[Execute]
    E --> V[Value]
    V --> En[Enable]
    En --> R[Replicate]

    subgraph Personal[Personal Capability]
        L
        E
        V
    end

    subgraph Leveraged[Leveraged Capability]
        En
        R
    end

docs.title: The Progression
docs.text: Stage Question Output Focus Learn What do I know? Knowledge Acquiring concepts and mental models Execute What can I do? Results Applying knowledge to produce work Value Can I independently create outcomes? Measurable outcomes Demonstrating trusted, independent capability Enable Can I make others successful? Capable people Developing others through mentoring and leadership Replicate Can I make success repeatable at scale? Systems Creating scalable capability through systems

docs.title: Personal Capability (L-E-V)
docs.text:
The first three stages focus on building individual capability:

  • Learn — Build the foundation of knowledge
  • Execute — Apply knowledge to produce results
  • Value — Demonstrate independent ability to create outcomes

At the end of Value, a practitioner can be trusted to work independently and deliver measurable results.

docs.title: Leveraged Capability (E-R)
docs.text:
The final two stages shift focus from personal output to multiplied impact:

  • Enable — Develop capability in others
  • Replicate — Create systems that scale capability

The AI-Era Extension

Classical frameworks like Dreyfus stop at "Expert." LEVER adds Enable and Replicate to capture how capability multiplies—especially important in the AI era where one person can leverage agents, platforms, and automation.

docs.title: Output Evolution
docs.text:
Each stage produces a different type of output:

Stage Output Example Learn Knowledge Understanding Kubernetes concepts Execute Work Deploying an application Value Outcomes Improving system reliability by 40% Enable People Mentoring three engineers to senior level Replicate Systems Creating a platform used by 50 teams

docs.title: Learn
docs.text:
• Studies concepts and terminology
  • Follows instructions and tutorials
  • Asks questions to understand
  • Builds foundational mental models

docs.title: Execute
docs.text:
• Completes assigned tasks
  • Applies learned techniques
  • Requires supervision and guidance
  • Produces work product with support

docs.title: Value
docs.text:
• Works independently
  • Makes sound decisions
  • Delivers measurable outcomes
  • Earns trust through results
  • Solves problems autonomously

docs.title: Enable
docs.text:
• Mentors and coaches others
  • Teaches effectively
  • Builds team capability
  • Multiplies impact through people
  • Creates psychological safety

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
• Creates reusable frameworks
  • Builds platforms and tools
  • Defines standards and best practices
  • Develops AI agents and automation
  • Establishes institutions and communities
  • Scales impact through systems

docs.title: Stage Transitions
docs.text:
Each transition represents a fundamental shift:

Transition Shift Learn → Execute From theory to practice Execute → Value From supervised to independent Value → Enable From individual to team focus Enable → Replicate From people to systems

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Explore each stage in detail:

  • Learn — Knowledge acquisition
  • Execute — Skill application
  • Value — Independent outcomes
  • Enable — Developing others
  • Replicate — System creation

docs.title: Enable
docs.text:
Stage 4 of 5 • Leveraged Capability

Core Question

Can I make others successful?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Enable stage marks the transition from personal capability to leveraged capability. Output shifts from individual work to developing others. Practitioners at this stage mentor, coach, teach, and lead—their success is measured by the success of others.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Capable People — Developed team members, mentored practitioners, built leadership.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Mentors and coaches others
  • Teaches effectively
  • Builds team capability
  • Multiplies impact through people
  • Creates psychological safety

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text:
Framework Corresponding Stage(s) Bloom's Taxonomy Create Dreyfus Model Master (extended) Shu-Ha-Ri Ri (離) Apprenticeship Lead, Teach Conscious Competence Reflective Mastery

Beyond Classical Frameworks

Most classical frameworks don't explicitly model this stage. Dreyfus stops at Expert. Bloom focuses on cognitive mastery, not leadership. Enable is where LEVER extends beyond traditional models.

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Mentors junior builders
  • Creates learning opportunities for the team
  • Builds high-performing product teams
  • Establishes product practices

docs.title: Software Engineer
docs.text:
• Sets technical direction for the team
  • Mentors multiple engineers
  • Leads technical initiatives
  • Runs knowledge-sharing sessions

docs.title: Evidence of Enabling
docs.text:
• Team members grow visibly under their guidance
  • Creates repeatable coaching approaches
  • Team succeeds when they're absent
  • Others seek them out for mentorship
  • Builds succession for their own role

docs.title: The Mindset Shift
docs.text:
Value Mindset Enable Mindset How can I deliver? How can I help others deliver? What's my output? What's my team's output? Am I succeeding? Are others succeeding? My skills Team capabilities

The Enable Test

You've truly enabled when your team succeeds without you.

docs.title: Forms of Enabling
docs.text: Enable happens through multiple channels:

docs.title: 1:1 Mentoring
docs.text: Direct coaching and guidance for individuals.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text: Structured knowledge transfer to groups.

docs.title: Leading
docs.text: Guiding teams toward outcomes.

docs.title: Culture Building
docs.text: Creating environments where others can grow.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Doing vs Developing

Taking over tasks instead of coaching others through them. "Let me just do it" undermines growth.

Hoarding Knowledge

Not sharing expertise, context, or decision-making. Enablers are generous with information.

Micromanagement

Over-controlling rather than empowering. Enable requires trust and tolerance for learning mistakes.

docs.title: Transition to Replicate
docs.text:
A practitioner is ready to transition to Replicate when they:

  • Have developed multiple successful team members
  • See patterns in how capability develops
  • Recognize limits of 1:1 enabling
  • Want to scale impact beyond direct relationships

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Enable is where impact starts to multiply—but it's bounded by human relationships. A great enabler might develop 10-20 people in their career. Replicate asks: can you create systems that multiply capability without your direct involvement?

In the AI era, enabling becomes both easier (AI can augment learning) and more important (humans need to develop judgment that AI lacks).

docs.title: Execute
docs.text:
Stage 2 of 5 • Personal Capability

Core Question

What can I do?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Execute stage is about applying knowledge to produce work. Practitioners move from theory to practice, completing tasks with guidance and supervision. This is where knowledge becomes skill.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Results — Completed work, delivered tasks, tangible outputs.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Completes assigned tasks
  • Applies learned techniques
  • Requires supervision and guidance
  • Produces work product with support

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text: Framework Corresponding Stage(s) Bloom's Taxonomy Apply Dreyfus Model Advanced Beginner, Competent Shu-Ha-Ri Shu (守) Apprenticeship Assist, Perform Conscious Competence Conscious Competence

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Creates functional prototypes
  • Uses AI tools to accelerate development
  • Completes features with supervision
  • Participates in product delivery

docs.title: Software Engineer
docs.text:
• Completes assigned tasks
  • Writes code that passes review
  • Debugs issues with support
  • Contributes to team projects

docs.title: Evidence of Execution
docs.text:
• Delivers assigned work on time
  • Code/work passes peer review
  • Can follow established processes
  • Shows improving efficiency over time

docs.title: The Supervision Gradient
docs.text:
Execute spans a range of independence:

Early Execute Late Execute Needs detailed instructions Understands intent Requires frequent check-ins Self-manages daily work Asks "how" questions Asks "why" questions Follows patterns exactly Recognizes when patterns apply

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Dependency on Instructions

Waiting for detailed specifications rather than developing judgment. The goal is to grow toward independence.

Avoiding Feedback

Not seeking code reviews or input. Feedback accelerates skill development.

Premature Independence

Trying to work alone before having sufficient skill. Execution improves through collaboration.

docs.title: Transition to Value
docs.text:
A practitioner is ready to transition to Value when they:

  • Can complete work without constant supervision
  • Make reasonable decisions independently
  • Understand trade-offs in their domain
  • Have demonstrated reliable delivery

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Execution is deliberate and effortful. It requires conscious attention and often feels slow. This is normal—automaticity comes later.

In the AI era, execution can be augmented by AI assistants. A practitioner who learns to leverage AI tools can accelerate their path from Execute to Value, but the fundamental skill development still requires practice.

docs.title: Learn
docs.text:
Stage 1 of 5 • Personal Capability

Core Question

What do I know?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Learn stage is about acquiring knowledge through study, observation, and instruction. Practitioners build vocabulary, concepts, and mental models that form the foundation for all subsequent capability development.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Knowledge — Understanding of concepts, terminology, principles, and frameworks.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Studies concepts and terminology
  • Follows instructions and tutorials
  • Asks questions to understand
  • Builds foundational mental models

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text: Framework Corresponding Stage(s) Bloom's Taxonomy Remember, Understand Dreyfus Model Novice Shu-Ha-Ri Shu (守) Apprenticeship Observe Conscious Competence Unconscious Incompetence → Conscious Incompetence

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Completes relevant courses and certifications
  • Studies successful products and case studies
  • Learns technical fundamentals (coding, AI, tools)
  • Understands business models and metrics

docs.title: Software Engineer
docs.text:
• Completes coding tutorials and courses
  • Understands basic CS concepts
  • Learns team tools and practices
  • Studies documentation and codebases

docs.title: Evidence of Learning
docs.text:
• Can explain concepts in their own words
  • Passes knowledge assessments
  • Completes structured learning programs
  • Demonstrates growing vocabulary

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Tutorial Hell

Getting stuck in endless learning without moving to execution. Learning should be purposeful and time-boxed.

Passive Consumption

Watching videos or reading without active engagement. True learning requires practice and reflection.

docs.title: Transition to Execute
docs.text:
A practitioner is ready to transition to Execute when they:

  • Have sufficient foundational knowledge
  • Can follow instructions to complete tasks
  • Understand the domain vocabulary
  • Are ready to apply knowledge with guidance

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Learning is necessary but not sufficient. The goal is not to accumulate knowledge but to build a foundation for doing.

In the AI era, learning can be accelerated through AI assistants that provide just-in-time knowledge. But deep understanding still requires active engagement and practice.

docs.title: Replicate
docs.text:
Stage 5 of 5 • Leveraged Capability

Core Question

Can I make success repeatable at scale?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Replicate stage represents the highest level of capability multiplication. Practitioners at this stage create systems—platforms, standards, frameworks, AI agents, automation, and institutions—that enable many others to succeed. Impact scales beyond direct human relationships.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Systems — Platforms, frameworks, standards, AI agents, automation, institutions.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Creates reusable frameworks
  • Builds platforms and tools
  • Defines standards and best practices
  • Develops AI agents and automation
  • Establishes institutions and communities
  • Scales impact through systems

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text:
Framework Corresponding Stage(s) Bloom's Taxonomy Beyond Bloom Dreyfus Model Teacher (extended), not in original Shu-Ha-Ri Ri (離) — creating new schools Apprenticeship Master of masters Conscious Competence Not addressed

The AI-Era Extension

Replicate is where LEVER makes its novel contribution. Classical frameworks weren't designed for an era where one person can create AI agents, platforms, and automation that multiply capability at scale.

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Creates reusable product frameworks and playbooks
  • Builds AI-powered product tools
  • Defines organizational product standards
  • Establishes product communities and guilds

docs.title: Software Engineer
docs.text:
• Creates reusable platforms used across teams
  • Defines engineering standards adopted organization-wide
  • Builds tools and automation that multiply productivity
  • Establishes technical communities and knowledge bases

docs.title: Evidence of Replication
docs.text:
• Systems continue working without direct involvement
  • Others build on foundations they've created
  • Impact measurable in multiples (10x, 100x)
  • Leaves lasting organizational or industry imprint
  • Creates things that outlive their direct tenure

docs.title: Platforms
docs.text:
Internal or external platforms that enable others to build.

Person → Platform → Many Builders → Many Products

docs.title: Frameworks
docs.text:
Methodologies, processes, and mental models that guide others.

Person → Framework → Many Teams → Consistent Excellence

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
Specifications, best practices, and norms that create alignment.

Person → Standard → Industry → Interoperability

docs.title: AI Agents
docs.text:
Automated systems that perform or augment work.

Person → Agent → Repeated Tasks → Scale

docs.title: Institutions
docs.text:
Organizations, communities, and movements that persist.

Person → Institution → Community → Ongoing Impact

docs.title: Historical Examples
docs.text: Person Replication W. Edwards Deming Quality frameworks used by millions Kent Beck XP, TDD methodologies Jeff Bezos PR/FAQ, Working Backwards Peter Drucker Management as a discipline Linus Torvalds Linux, Git

docs.title: The Multiplication Spectrum
docs.text: Stage Multiplication Value 1x (individual) Enable 10x (team/mentees) Replicate 100x+ (systems)

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Over-Engineering

Building complex systems when simple ones suffice. Not every framework needs to be a platform.

Premature Abstraction

Creating standards before patterns are proven. Replicate what works, not what might work.

Abandonment

Creating systems but not maintaining or evolving them. Living systems need stewardship.

Not-Invented-Here

Building from scratch when existing systems could be extended. Replication includes adoption.

docs.title: The AI-Era Opportunity
docs.text:
Why This Matters Now

AI dramatically lowers the cost of creating replicable systems:

  • AI agents can perform tasks at scale
  • Code generation accelerates platform building
  • Documentation can be auto-generated and maintained
  • Personalization allows one system to serve many contexts

This means practitioners can reach Replicate earlier in their careers than ever before.

docs.title: Examples of Modern Replication
docs.text: Type Example AI Agent A code review agent used by 100 engineers Platform An internal developer platform serving 50 teams Framework A product discovery playbook adopted company-wide Standard An API specification enabling ecosystem growth Community A guild of 200 practitioners sharing knowledge

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Replication is the ultimate form of leverage. While Enable is bounded by human relationships, Replicate scales through systems. In the AI era, the question isn't whether you can teach others—it's whether you can create systems that multiply capability without your direct involvement.

The progression complete:

Learn (Know) → Execute (Do) → Value (Deliver) → Enable (Develop) → Replicate (Multiply)

docs.title: Value
docs.text:
Stage 3 of 5 • Personal Capability

Core Question

Can I independently create outcomes?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Value stage represents the transition from supervised execution to independent outcome creation. Practitioners at this stage don't just complete tasks—they deliver measurable value without oversight. They earn trust through results.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Measurable Outcomes — Business value, solved problems, achieved goals.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Works independently
  • Makes sound decisions
  • Delivers measurable outcomes
  • Earns trust through results
  • Solves problems autonomously

docs.title: Framework Mappings
docs.text: Framework Corresponding Stage(s) Bloom's Taxonomy Analyze, Evaluate Dreyfus Model Proficient, Expert Shu-Ha-Ri Ha (破) Apprenticeship Perform (mastery) Conscious Competence Unconscious Competence

docs.title: Product Builder
docs.text:
• Ships products without supervision
  • Drives measurable business outcomes
  • Makes sound product decisions
  • Owns the full product lifecycle

docs.title: Software Engineer
docs.text:
• Owns technical decisions
  • Delivers complex features end-to-end
  • Mentors junior engineers informally
  • Trusted to work independently

docs.title: Evidence of Value Creation
docs.text:
• Delivers outcomes, not just outputs
  • Requires minimal supervision
  • Makes decisions others trust
  • Has track record of success
  • Solves novel problems effectively

docs.title: The Outcome vs Output Distinction
docs.text:
Output (Execute) Outcome (Value) Shipped a feature Increased user engagement 25% Wrote documentation Reduced support tickets 40% Built an API Enabled 10 new integrations Completed the project Delivered business value

Key Shift

At Execute, you're measured by completing work. At Value, you're measured by the impact of that work.

docs.title: Why This Stage Matters
docs.text:
Value is often where many practitioners plateau. They become reliable executors but don't progress to multiplying their impact through others.

This is a stable equilibrium—a practitioner can have a successful career at Value without ever reaching Enable or Replicate.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Comfort Zone

Staying in individual contributor mode when capable of more. Some choose this; others get stuck.

Hero Culture

Over-indexing on personal delivery rather than team success. This limits future growth.

Measuring Activity

Confusing busyness with value. Hours worked ≠ outcomes delivered.

docs.title: Transition to Enable
docs.text:
A practitioner is ready to transition to Enable when they:

  • Have proven ability to deliver value independently
  • Begin informally mentoring others
  • See opportunities to multiply through people
  • Understand that their ceiling is limited as an individual

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Value is where expertise becomes trusted. But individual excellence has limits. The next stage asks: can you multiply that value through others?

In the AI era, Value becomes easier to achieve earlier in a career. AI tools can augment judgment and execution. But the fundamental question remains: can you create outcomes that matter?

docs.title: CLI
docs.text: The LEVER CLI provides command-line access to the framework specification.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
go install github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever/cmd/lever@latest

Or build from source:

git clone https://github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever
cd lever
go build -o lever ./cmd/lever

docs.title: lever stages
docs.text:
List all LEVER stages with their questions and outputs:

$ lever stages
1. Learn (learn)
   Question: Can I follow instructions?
   Output:   Knowledge

2. Execute (execute)
   Question: Can I complete tasks?
   Output:   Work

3. Value (value)
   Question: Can I independently create outcomes?
   Output:   Outcomes

4. Enable (enable)
   Question: Can I develop others?
   Output:   People

5. Replicate (replicate)
   Question: Can I multiply my impact?
   Output:   Systems

docs.title: lever spec
docs.text:
Output the complete LEVER specification as JSON:

$ lever spec > lever-spec.json

$ lever spec | jq '.stages[2]'
{
  "id": "value",
  "name": "Value",
  "order": 3,
  "question": "Can I independently create outcomes?",
  "output": "Outcomes",
  "description": "Independently creating outcomes that matter..."
}

docs.title: lever schema
docs.text:
Generate JSON Schema for the LEVER specification:

$ lever schema > lever.schema.json

$ lever schema | jq '.properties.stages'
{
  "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/Stage" },
  "type": "array"
}

docs.title: lever profile-schema
docs.text:
Generate JSON Schema for LEVER profiles:

$ lever profile-schema > profile.schema.json

docs.title: lever mappings
docs.text:
Output the framework mapping table as JSON:

$ lever mappings | jq '.rows[0]'
{
  "lever_stage": "learn",
  "cells": {
    "bloom": ["remember", "understand"],
    "dreyfus": ["novice"],
    "shu-ha-ri": ["shu"],
    "apprenticeship": ["observe"],
    "conscious-competence": ["unconscious-incompetence", "conscious-incompetence"]
  }
}

docs.title: lever version
docs.text:
Print the specification version:

$ lever version
0.1.0

2. Can I complete tasks?

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/validate.yml
jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: '1.22'
      - run: go install github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever/cmd/lever@latest
      - run: lever spec > spec.json
      - run: lever schema > schema.json

docs.title: Validation Script
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# validate-profile.sh

PROFILE_FILE=$1
SCHEMA_FILE=$(mktemp)

lever profile-schema > "$SCHEMA_FILE"

# Validate with ajv or similar
npx ajv validate -s "$SCHEMA_FILE" -d "$PROFILE_FILE"

rm "$SCHEMA_FILE"

docs.title: Help
docs.text:
$ lever
lever - LEVER Capability Framework CLI

Usage:
  lever <command>

Commands:
  schema          Generate JSON Schema for lever.Spec (stdout)
  profile-schema  Generate JSON Schema for lever.Profile (stdout)

docs.title: Go Library
docs.text: The LEVER Go library provides types and functions for working with the framework programmatically.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: go get github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "encoding/json"
    "fmt"

    "github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever/pkg/lever"
)

func main() {
    // Get all stages
    stages := lever.Stages()
    for _, s := range stages {
        fmt.Printf("%d. %s - %s\n", s.Order, s.Name, s.Question)
    }

    // Get complete specification
    spec := lever.NewSpec()
    data, _ := json.MarshalIndent(spec, "", "  ")
    fmt.Println(string(data))
}

docs.title: Stages
docs.text:
// Get all stages
stages := lever.Stages()

// Get a specific stage
stage := lever.StageByID(lever.StageValue)
fmt.Println(stage.Question) // "Can I independently create outcomes?"

// All stage IDs
ids := lever.AllStageIDs()
// []StageID{"learn", "execute", "value", "enable", "replicate"}

docs.title: Stage Constants
docs.text:
const (
    StageLearn     StageID = "learn"
    StageExecute   StageID = "execute"
    StageValue     StageID = "value"
    StageEnable    StageID = "enable"
    StageReplicate StageID = "replicate"
)

docs.title: Stage Type
docs.text:
type Stage struct {
    ID              StageID  `json:"id"`
    Name            string   `json:"name"`
    Order           int      `json:"order"`
    Question        string   `json:"question"`
    Output          string   `json:"output"`
    Description     string   `json:"description"`
    Characteristics []string `json:"characteristics,omitempty"`
}

docs.title: Classical Frameworks
docs.text:
// Get all frameworks
frameworks := lever.AllFrameworks()

// Get specific framework
bloom := lever.BloomTaxonomy()
dreyfus := lever.DreyfusModel()
shuHaRi := lever.ShuHaRi()
apprenticeship := lever.ApprenticeshipModel()
conscious := lever.ConsciousCompetenceModel()

// Get framework by ID
framework := lever.FrameworkByID(lever.FrameworkBloom)

docs.title: Framework Type
docs.text:
type Framework struct {
    ID           FrameworkID      `json:"id"`
    Name         string           `json:"name"`
    Description  string           `json:"description"`
    Origin       string           `json:"origin"`
    PrimaryFocus string           `json:"primary_focus"`
    Stages       []FrameworkStage `json:"stages"`
}

docs.title: Mappings
docs.text:
// Get all mappings
mappings := lever.AllMappings()

// Get specific framework mappings
bloomMappings := lever.BloomMappings()
dreyfusMappings := lever.DreyfusMappings()

// Build mapping table
table := lever.BuildMappingTable()
for _, row := range table.Rows {
    fmt.Printf("%s -> %v\n", row.LEVERStage, row.Cells)
}

docs.title: Mapping Types
docs.text:
type StageMapping struct {
    LEVERStage      StageID     `json:"lever_stage"`
    FrameworkID     FrameworkID `json:"framework_id"`
    FrameworkStages []string    `json:"framework_stages"`
    Notes           string      `json:"notes,omitempty"`
}

type MappingTable struct {
    Frameworks []FrameworkID `json:"frameworks"`
    Rows       []MappingRow  `json:"rows"`
}

docs.title: Profiles
docs.text:
// Reference profiles
profiles := lever.ReferenceProfiles()
productBuilder := lever.ProductBuilderProfile()
engineer := lever.EngineerProfile()

// Create custom profile
profile := lever.Profile{
    ID:          "my-domain",
    Name:        "My Domain",
    Description: "Custom LEVER interpretation",
    Domain:      "custom",
    StageInterpretations: []lever.StageInterpretation{
        {
            StageID:     lever.StageLearn,
            Title:       "Learner",
            Description: "Learning domain fundamentals",
            Evidence:    []string{"Completes training"},
        },
        // ... other stages
    },
}

docs.title: Profile Type
docs.text:
type Profile struct {
    ID                   ProfileID             `json:"id"`
    Name                 string                `json:"name"`
    Description          string                `json:"description"`
    Domain               string                `json:"domain"`
    StageInterpretations []StageInterpretation `json:"stage_interpretations"`
}

type StageInterpretation struct {
    StageID      StageID      `json:"stage_id"`
    Title        string       `json:"title,omitempty"`
    Description  string       `json:"description"`
    Competencies []Competency `json:"competencies,omitempty"`
    Evidence     []string     `json:"evidence,omitempty"`
    Examples     []string     `json:"examples,omitempty"`
}

docs.title: Complete Specification
docs.text:
// Get the complete spec
spec := lever.NewSpec()

// Access components
fmt.Println(spec.Version)      // "0.1.0"
fmt.Println(spec.Name)         // "LEVER Capability Framework"
fmt.Println(len(spec.Stages))  // 5
fmt.Println(len(spec.Frameworks)) // 5

// Export as JSON
data, err := json.Marshal(spec)

docs.title: Spec Type
docs.text:
type Spec struct {
    Version           string             `json:"version"`
    Name              string             `json:"name"`
    Description       string             `json:"description"`
    Stages            []Stage            `json:"stages"`
    Frameworks        []Framework        `json:"frameworks"`
    Mappings          []FrameworkMappings `json:"mappings"`
    MappingTable      MappingTable       `json:"mapping_table"`
    ReferenceProfiles []Profile          `json:"reference_profiles,omitempty"`
}

docs.title: Stage Progression
docs.text:
// Get progression split
prog := lever.GetStageProgression()
fmt.Println(prog.Personal)  // [learn, execute, value]
fmt.Println(prog.Leveraged) // [enable, replicate]

// Output progression
output := lever.OutputProgression[lever.StageValue]
fmt.Println(output) // "Outcomes"

docs.title: Embedded JSON Schema
docs.text:
import "github.com/ProductBuildersHQ/lever/schema"

// Access embedded schemas
leverSchema := schema.LeverSchema
profileSchema := schema.ProfileSchema

// Use for validation
var spec lever.Spec
// ... validate against leverSchema

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Most functions return values directly since they work with compile-time constants:

// Returns nil if not found
stage := lever.StageByID("invalid")
if stage == nil {
    // Handle unknown stage
}

framework := lever.FrameworkByID("unknown")
if framework == nil {
    // Handle unknown framework
}

docs.title: TypeScript / Zod
docs.text: LEVER provides TypeScript types and Zod schemas for runtime validation.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
npm install zod

Copy the LEVER type files to your project:

  • ts/lever.zod.ts — Zod schemas with inferred types
  • ts/lever.d.ts — Pure TypeScript interfaces

docs.title: Using Zod Schemas
docs.text:
import {
  stageIdSchema,
  specSchema,
  profileSchema,
  STAGES,
  STAGE_PROGRESSION
} from './lever.zod';

// Validate a stage ID
const result = stageIdSchema.safeParse('value');
if (result.success) {
  console.log(`Valid stage: ${result.data}`);
}

// Validate a complete spec
const specData = await fetch('/api/lever-spec').then(r => r.json());
const spec = specSchema.parse(specData);
console.log(`LEVER v${spec.version}`);

// Use constants
console.log(STAGES); // ['learn', 'execute', 'value', 'enable', 'replicate']
console.log(STAGE_PROGRESSION.personal);  // ['learn', 'execute', 'value']
console.log(STAGE_PROGRESSION.leveraged); // ['enable', 'replicate']

docs.title: Stage Types
docs.text:
import type { StageId, Stage } from './lever.zod';

// StageId is a union type
type StageId = 'learn' | 'execute' | 'value' | 'enable' | 'replicate';

// Stage interface
interface Stage {
  id: StageId;
  name: string;
  order: number;
  question: string;
  output: string;
  description: string;
  characteristics?: string[];
}

docs.title: Framework Types
docs.text:
import type { FrameworkId, Framework, FrameworkStage } from './lever.zod';

type FrameworkId =
  | 'bloom'
  | 'dreyfus'
  | 'shu-ha-ri'
  | 'apprenticeship'
  | 'conscious-competence';

interface Framework {
  id: FrameworkId;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  origin: string;
  primary_focus: string;
  stages: FrameworkStage[];
}

docs.title: Profile Types
docs.text:
import type { Profile, StageInterpretation, Competency } from './lever.zod';

interface Profile {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  domain: string;
  stage_interpretations: StageInterpretation[];
}

interface StageInterpretation {
  stage_id: StageId;
  title?: string;
  description: string;
  competencies?: Competency[];
  evidence?: string[];
  examples?: string[];
}

docs.title: Available Schemas
docs.text: Schema Description stageIdSchema LEVER stage identifier enum stageSchema Complete stage definition frameworkIdSchema Classical framework identifier enum frameworkSchema Classical framework definition stageMappingSchema LEVER to framework stage mapping mappingTableSchema Full mapping matrix profileSchema Domain-specific profile specSchema Complete LEVER specification

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
import {
  STAGES,
  FRAMEWORKS,
  STAGE_PROGRESSION,
  OUTPUT_PROGRESSION
} from './lever.zod';

// All stage IDs in order
STAGES // ['learn', 'execute', 'value', 'enable', 'replicate']

// All framework IDs
FRAMEWORKS // ['bloom', 'dreyfus', 'shu-ha-ri', 'apprenticeship', 'conscious-competence']

// Stage categories
STAGE_PROGRESSION.personal  // ['learn', 'execute', 'value']
STAGE_PROGRESSION.leveraged // ['enable', 'replicate']

// Output by stage
OUTPUT_PROGRESSION.learn     // 'Knowledge'
OUTPUT_PROGRESSION.execute   // 'Work'
OUTPUT_PROGRESSION.value     // 'Outcomes'
OUTPUT_PROGRESSION.enable    // 'People'
OUTPUT_PROGRESSION.replicate // 'Systems'

docs.title: Validating User Input
docs.text:
import { stageIdSchema } from './lever.zod';

function setUserStage(input: unknown) {
  const result = stageIdSchema.safeParse(input);
  if (!result.success) {
    console.error('Invalid stage:', result.error.issues);
    return null;
  }
  return result.data;
}

setUserStage('value');   // Returns 'value'
setUserStage('invalid'); // Returns null, logs error

docs.title: Validating API Responses
docs.text:
import { specSchema } from './lever.zod';

async function fetchLeverSpec(): Promise<Spec | null> {
  const response = await fetch('/api/lever');
  const data = await response.json();

  const result = specSchema.safeParse(data);
  if (!result.success) {
    console.error('Invalid spec:', result.error.format());
    return null;
  }

  return result.data;
}

docs.title: Creating Custom Profiles
docs.text:
import { profileSchema, type Profile } from './lever.zod';

const myProfile: Profile = {
  id: 'data-scientist',
  name: 'Data Scientist',
  description: 'LEVER for data science roles',
  domain: 'data',
  stage_interpretations: [
    {
      stage_id: 'learn',
      title: 'Analyst',
      description: 'Learning data fundamentals',
      evidence: ['Completes statistics courses', 'Learns Python/R'],
    },
    // ... other stages
  ],
};

// Validate the profile
const validated = profileSchema.parse(myProfile);

docs.title: Pure TypeScript (No Zod)
docs.text:
If you don't need runtime validation, use the pure TypeScript interfaces:

import type {
  StageId,
  Stage,
  Framework,
  Profile,
  Spec
} from './lever.d';

// Type-safe without Zod dependency
function processStage(stage: Stage) {
  console.log(`${stage.order}. ${stage.name}`);
}

docs.title: Type Synchronization
docs.text:
LEVER types are maintained in sync with Go types:

  1. Go structs are the source of truth (pkg/lever/)
  2. JSON Schema is generated from Go (schema/)
  3. TypeScript/Zod is manually synchronized (ts/)

When Go types change, update TypeScript files to match.
